topics.title: Bio Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bio Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Bio Amplifier

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bio Amplifier (Biomedical Amplifier / Biopotential Amplifier):** A precision electronic amplifier specifically engineered to detect, condition, and amplify low-amplitude bioelectric signals originating from living tissues (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) while rejecting noise, interference, and common-mode voltages. It is the **front-end analog stage** of virtually every biomedical instrument.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCBRL407 – Biomedical Electronics Lab**, the bio amplifier experiment is typically built around an **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** topology (e.g., the classic 3-op-amp configuration built from the INA/AD620 family or a discrete three-Op-Amp realization) because it uniquely satisfies all the demanding requirements of biopotential acquisition.

### 1.1.1 The Five Mandatory Performance Parameters

A bio amplifier is judged by **five** non-negotiable performance parameters as per KTU 2024 evaluation rubrics:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Biopotential) |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------------------------|
| 1 | Input Signal Range | $V_{in}$ | **0.1 mV – 5 mV** (ECG); 1–10 µV (EEG) |
| 2 | Gain (Mid-band) | $A_v$ | **100 – 10,000** (40–80 dB) |
| 3 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | **≥ 80 dB** (ideally > 100 dB) |
| 4 | Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | **≥ 10 MΩ** (typically 10 GΩ) |
| 5 | Frequency Bandwidth | $BW$ | **0.05 Hz – 1.5 kHz** (ECG); 0.5–150 Hz (EEG) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microphone in a Thunderstorm" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine trying to hear a friend whisper (≈ 1 mV bio signal) inside a stadium during a thunderstorm (≈ 50/60 Hz mains pickup + muscle noise + electrode offset). A normal audio amplifier would just blow up the thunder into a roar. A **bio amplifier** is like a *supersonic, directional, noise-cancelling microphone* that:
> 1. Hears the whisper with **10,000× volume boost** (high gain).
> 2. **Ignores** the thunder equally hitting both ears (high CMRR).
> 3. Sits on the speaker with a **10 MΩ cushion** so it never distorts the whisper source (high $Z_{in}$).
> 4. Lets the deep bass (0.05 Hz) and the high-pitch shrieks (1.5 kHz) pass, but blocks DC drift and RF garbage (band-pass filtering).

This is **exactly** what the lab experiment in Module 2 demonstrates: a working bio amplifier that converts invisible µV–mV bio-signals into a clean 1–5 V waveform you can see on an oscilloscope.

### 1.2.1 Why a Normal Op-Amp Will NOT Work

A plain op-amp such as µA741 is **inadequate** for biopotentials because:
- Its $Z_{in}$ (~2 MΩ) **loads the high-impedance electrode–skin interface** ($Z_{skin} \approx$ 10–100 kΩ → voltage divider losses).
- Its CMRR (~70 dB) is **insufficient** to reject the 50 Hz mains interference present on the body.
- It has **no adjustable gain** without sacrificing bandwidth.
- Its input bias currents create **DC offset** that saturates the next stage.

Hence KTU mandates the **3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier** as the canonical bio-amp realization.

---

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2 — *Biomedical Signal Acquisition & Conditioning*), students are expected to:
> - Design, build, and test a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** on breadboard/PCB.
> - Measure gain, CMRR, input impedance, and bandwidth using **function generator + oscilloscope + decade resistance box**.
> - (Optional) Acquire a real **ECG/EMG signal** through surface electrodes and visualize it on CRO/PC.
> - Submit a **lab record** with circuit diagram, theoretical vs. measured values, and error analysis.

The experiment is the **gateway experiment** of the biomedical lab — every other experiment (ECG, EMG, pulse oximeter) depends on understanding this block.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Classic 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

The instrumentation amplifier is a **two-stage, fully-differential amplifier** built from three op-amps. It is the *de-facto standard* for bio-signal front-ends.

### 2.1.1 Stage-Wise Operational Breakdown

**STAGE 1 — The Buffer / Difference Amplifier Pair (Op-Amps A1 & A2):**
- A1 and A2 form a **symmetric, non-inverting buffer pair** sharing a single gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs.
- Both op-amps receive the same differential input $(V_1 - V_2)$ but **share the same common-mode voltage $V_{CM}$**.
- Because of the virtual-short property, the voltage across $R_G$ equals $V_1 - V_2$.
- The same current $\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$ therefore flows through the two **feedback resistors $R_F$** of A1 and A2.
- This current is the **same in both arms** → the circuit becomes a *current-mode* subtractor, giving perfect common-mode rejection *inherently* (no resistor matching required at this stage).
- Output of stage 1: $V_{A1} - V_{A2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_F}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$

**STAGE 2 — The Unity-Gain Difference Amplifier (Op-Amp A3 with $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$):**
- A3 converts the differential output of stage 1 into a **single-ended, ground-referenced output**.
- Resistors must satisfy the **matching condition** $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$ to preserve CMRR.
- Stage 2 gain: $\frac{R_2}{R_1}$.
- Total instrumentation amplifier gain: $A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_F}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

- **Why two buffer op-amps at the input?** They provide *infinite* (or > 10 GΩ) input impedance to **both** input terminals — so the bio-signal source sees no loading.
- **Why a single $R_G$ between the two inverting inputs?** It makes the two input stages a **matched pair**, so any common-mode signal is automatically subtracted *before* it can be amplified.
- **Why a difference amplifier for stage 2?** It eliminates the common-mode voltage that survived stage 1, with rejection ratio determined by the resistor matching tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Utility:** This same IA topology is the **front-end of every medical-grade ECG machine, EEG recorder, patient monitor, defibrillator, and pulse oximeter** in clinical use worldwide. The AD620, INA128, INA333, and INA118 are commercial IC realizations of this exact 3-op-amp topology.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-essential. Memorize the boxed equations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Total Gain** | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_F}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Dimensionless; set by $R_G$ alone (usually) |
| 2 | Differential Gain of Stage 1 | $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R_F}{R_G}$ | Range: 1 to 1000 |
| 3 | Differential Gain of Stage 2 | $A_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Typically 1, 5, or 10 |
| 4 | **CMRR (in dB)** | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \dfrac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}} \right\vert$ | Target ≥ 80 dB |
| 5 | CMRR due to Resistor Mismatch | $\text{CMRR} \approx \dfrac{1}{4 \cdot \Delta R / R}$ | $\Delta R$ = mismatch between $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ |
| 6 | **Gain-Bandwidth Product** | $A_v \cdot BW = \text{GBW (constant)}$ | For TL081/741: GBW ≈ 1 MHz |
| 7 | **Noise** | $V_{n,out} = A_v \cdot \sqrt{e_n^2 + i_n^2 \cdot R_{source}^2}$ | $e_n$ in nV/√Hz, $i_n$ in pA/√Hz |
| 8 | **Input Impedance (Ideal)** | $Z_{in} \approx Z_{in,opamp} \cdot A_1$ | Practically ≥ 10 GΩ |
| 9 | **Lower Cutoff (HPF)** | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Choose 0.05 Hz (ECG) or 0.5 Hz (EEG) |
| 10 | **Upper Cutoff (LPF)** | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Choose 150 Hz (EEG) or 1500 Hz (EMG) |
| 11 | **Output Voltage** | $V_{out} = A_v \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$ | Single-ended, ground-referenced |
| 12 | **DC Offset (per stage)** | $V_{offset,out} = A_v \cdot V_{os,input}$ | Choose op-amps with low $V_{os}$ (e.g., OP07, AD620) |
| 13 | **Slew Rate Requirement** | $SR \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_{peak}$ | For ECG: SR ≥ 0.5 V/µs suffices |
| 14 | **Power-Line Rejection** | $\text{PSRR} = 20 \log \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta V_{supply}}{\Delta V_{out}} \right\vert$ | ≥ 80 dB at 50 Hz |
| 15 | **Safety — Leakage Current** | $I_{leak} \leq 10 \;\mu A$ (Type CF) | Mandated by IEC 60601-1 |

### 2.2.1 Numerical Reference — A Typical ECG Bio-Amplifier Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Reference (Memorize):**
> - $R_F = 47 \;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1 \;\text{k}\Omega$ → $A_1 = 1 + 2(47/1) = 95$
> - $R_1 = R_3 = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_4 = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$ → $A_2 = 1$
> - **Total Gain $A_v = 95$** (so a 1 mV ECG → 95 mV output; often a second stage raises total to ~1000)
> - With 0.1% resistors, $\text{CMRR} \approx 20 \log(1/(4 \times 0.001)) = 20 \log(250) = 48 \;\text{dB}$ from mismatch alone — hence **precision resistor networks or laser-trimmed ICs** are used clinically.

### 2.2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Medical Devices:** Patient monitors, ECG/EEG/EMG machines, defibrillators, fetal heart monitors.
- **Research:** Brain–computer interfaces (BCI), neural prosthetics, cochlear implants.
- **Wearable Tech:** Smartwatches (PPG/ECG), fitness bands, ECG patches (AliveCor, Apple Watch).
- **Industrial Bio:** Ergonomics (sEMG for muscle fatigue), sports science, animal veterinary monitors.
- **Safety-Critical:** Aviation pilot ECG, military battlefield triage, astronaut health monitoring on ISS.

The instrumentation amplifier is the **single most-used analog building block** in all of biomedical engineering, and the KTU lab experiment is its first hands-on realization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Total Gain of the 3-Op-Amp IA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following derivation is the KTU 2024 board-exam "showpiece" derivation. It is expected to be reproducible step-by-step in the answer script.**

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup & Node Labeling

Consider the 3-op-amp IA with:
- Non-inverting inputs: $V_1$ (at A1 "+") and $V_2$ (at A2 "+").
- Gain resistor: $R_G$ connecting inverting inputs of A1 and A2.
- Feedback resistors: $R_F$ in the feedback path of A1 and A2.
- Difference amplifier (A3): four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ with $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$.

Let the inverting input nodes of A1 and A2 be **node A** and **node B** respectively.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Stage 1 Gain

**Step 1 — Apply the virtual-short principle at A1 and A2.**

Because A1 and A2 are ideal op-amps operating in negative feedback, the differential input voltage to each is zero. Therefore:
- Voltage at node A = $V_1$
- Voltage at node B = $V_2$

**Step 2 — Express the voltage across the gain resistor $R_G$.**

The voltage difference between node A and node B equals the differential input:
$$
V_A - V_B = V_1 - V_2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the current flowing through $R_G$.**

By Ohm's law applied across $R_G$:
$$
I_{R_G} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that this same current must flow through the two $R_F$ resistors.**

Because no current enters the ideal inverting inputs of A1 and A2, the current $I_{R_G}$ continues unchanged through $R_F$ of A1 (from node A to output of A1) and through $R_F$ of A2 (from node B to output of A2). This is the **key current-mode trick** that gives the IA its excellent common-mode rejection.

**Step 5 — Compute the output voltages of A1 and A2.**

The voltage drop across each $R_F$ is $I_{R_G} \cdot R_F$. Therefore the output of A1 is *one $R_F$-drop above* $V_1$, and the output of A2 is *one $R_F$-drop below* $V_2$:

$$
V_{A1} = V_1 + I_{R_G} \cdot R_F = V_1 + \frac{R_F}{R_G} (V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
V_{A2} = V_2 - I_{R_G} \cdot R_F = V_2 - \frac{R_F}{R_G} (V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 6 — Subtract to find the differential output of Stage 1.**

$$
V_{A1} - V_{A2} = \left(V_1 - V_2\right) + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G} \left(V_1 - V_2\right)
$$

$$
V_{A1} - V_{A2} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G}\right) \left(V_1 - V_2\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_1 = 1 + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G}\,}
$$

This is the **Stage 1 gain** of the instrumentation amplifier.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Stage 2 Gain

**Step 7 — Apply KCL at the inverting input of A3 (call it node C).**

Let the output of the difference amplifier be $V_{out}$. The non-inverting input of A3 (call it node D) is connected to the junction of $R_3$ and $R_4$, which receives $V_{A2}$. Then:
$$
V_D = V_{A2} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
$$

By the virtual-short principle at A3:
$$
V_C = V_D
$$

KCL at node C (current into node = 0):
$$
\frac{V_{A1} - V_C}{R_1} = \frac{V_C - V_{out}}{R_2}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $V_C = V_D$ and solve for $V_{out}$.**

After algebraic substitution (shown in full) and using the matching conditions $R_1 = R_3 = R_a$ and $R_2 = R_4 = R_b$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \left(V_{A1} - V_{A2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_2 = \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_b}{R_a}\,}
$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Overall Transfer Function

**Step 9 — Multiply the two stage gains.**

$$
A_v = A_1 \cdot A_2 = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \left(V_1 - V_2\right)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024 Board Exam:** The gain of the entire bio-amplifier is **controlled by a SINGLE resistor $R_G$**. The student can vary the gain (e.g., 10, 100, 1000) by swapping $R_G$ alone — this is exactly the experiment's standard procedure.

### 3.1.5 CMRR Derivation (Stage 2 Resistor Mismatch)

If the four resistors of the difference amplifier are *not* perfectly matched, let $R_2 = R_1 (1 + \delta_1)$ and $R_4 = R_3 (1 + \delta_2)$. Then the common-mode gain becomes:

$$
A_{CM} \approx \frac{\delta_1 + \delta_2}{4}
$$

The differential-mode gain $A_{DM} = R_2 / R_1$. The CMRR is:

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}} \approx \frac{4}{\delta_1 + \delta_2}
$$

For 0.1% resistors ($\delta \approx 0.001$ each):
$$
\text{CMRR} \approx \frac{4}{0.002} = 2000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log 2000 \approx 66 \;\text{dB}
$$

For 0.01% resistors (precision networks or laser-trimmed ICs):
$$
\text{CMRR} \approx 86 \;\text{dB} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{CMRR}_{dB} \approx 98 \;\text{dB}
$$

This is **why clinical IA chips (AD620, INA128) are laser-trimmed** to > 100 dB CMRR.

### 3.1.6 Frequency Response Derivation

With capacitive coupling at the input (HPF capacitor $C_{in}$ in series with $R_F$ or $R_G$):

$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in}}
$$

For ECG ($f_L = 0.05$ Hz) with $R_{eq} = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$:
$$
C_{in} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 0.05} \approx 31.8 \;\mu\text{F} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{use } 33 \;\mu\text{F}
$$

For EEG ($f_L = 0.5$ Hz) with $R_{eq} = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$:
$$
C_{in} \approx 3.18 \;\mu\text{F} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{use } 3.3 \;\mu\text{F}
$$

The upper cutoff is set by a feedback capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_F$ (or by the op-amp's GBW limit):
$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_F C_f} \;\;\;\text{or}\;\;\; f_H = \frac{\text{GBW}}{A_v}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bio Amplifier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Bio Source Electrode 1] --> padA[Electrode Skin Interface Zskin 10 to 100 kohm]
    padA --> prot1[Input Protection Diode Clamp plus ESD]
    prot1 --> hpA[AC Coupling HPF fc 0.05 Hz]
    hpA --> buf1A[Opamp A1 Buffer with Gain 1 plus 2RF by RG]
    buf1A --> rg[Centre Tap Resistor RG Gain Set]
    rg --> buf1B[Opamp A2 Symmetric Buffer]
    prot2[Input Protection Diode Clamp plus ESD] --> buf1B
    srcB[Bio Source Electrode 2] --> padB[Electrode Skin Interface Zskin 10 to 100 kohm]
    padB --> prot2
    hpB[AC Coupling HPF fc 0.05 Hz] --> buf1B
    buf1A --> diff[Opamp A3 Difference Amplifier R2 by R1]
    buf1B --> diff
    diff --> lpf[Second Order LPF fc 150 Hz or 1.5 kHz]
    lpf --> gain[Optional Second Gain Stage Av 10]
    gain --> iso[Isolation Barrier IEC 60601]
    iso --> outA[Single Ended Output to ADC plus Display]
    iso --> isoMon[Patient Leakage Current Monitor less than 10 microamp]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Detailed Schematic — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Input V1 Differential Bio Signal] --> nA1[Node A at inverting input of A1]
    V2[Input V2 Differential Bio Signal] --> nB2[Node B at inverting input of A2]
    nA1 --> RFB1[Feedback Resistor RF in A1 path]
    RFB1 --> OA1[Output of A1 equals V1 plus I times RF]
    nB2 --> RFB2[Feedback Resistor RF in A2 path]
    RFB2 --> OA2[Output of A2 equals V2 minus I times RF]
    nA1 --> RG[Gain Resistor RG between A and B]
    RG --> nB2
    OA1 --> R1[R1 into inverting of A3]
    R1 --> nC[Node C inverting of A3]
    OA2 --> R3[R3 into non inverting of A3]
    R3 --> nD[Node D non inverting of A3]
    nD --> R4[R4 from node D to ground]
    nC --> R2[R2 feedback of A3]
    R2 --> nOUT[Output Node Vout]
    nOUT --> A3[Opamp A3 Difference Subtractor]
    A3 --> nOUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Experimental Test-Bench Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FG[Function Generator Sine Input 1 kHz 10 mVpp Differential] --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier on Breadboard]
    FG2[Common Mode Source 1 kHz 1 Vpp applied to both inputs] --> IA
    IA --> CRO[Digital Storage Oscilloscope Channel 1 for Output]
    IA --> RMS[True RMS Multimeter for Output Amplitude]
    FG --> CRO2[Reference Channel 2 on Oscilloscope]
    IA --> DAQ[Optional DAQ to PC for CSV Logging]
    PS[Plus 15V and Minus 15V Dual Power Supply] --> IA
```

## 4.4 Reading the Diagrams

- **Diagram 4.1** shows the *system-level* signal flow: electrode → protection → AC coupling → 3-op-amp IA → filter → gain → isolation → output. This is the canonical biomedical signal-acquisition chain.
- **Diagram 4.2** is the *circuit-level* schematic of the 3-op-amp IA — note the **single gain-set resistor $R_G$** between the two inverting inputs; this is the **"volume knob"** of the bio-amplifier.
- **Diagram 4.3** is the *lab test bench* — students feed a known sine wave from the function generator and measure the gain/CMRR/bandwidth on the oscilloscope and multimeter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** The CMRR measurement is done by **ganging the two inputs together** (so $V_1 = V_2 = V_{CM}$) and applying a large common-mode sine wave (e.g., 1 Vpp at 50 Hz). The output should be ideally zero; whatever small output remains, divided by the input, gives $A_{CM}$. Then CMRR (dB) = $20 \log(A_{DM} / A_{CM})$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> Each question tests a **Revised Bloom's L2 (Understand)** cognitive level. Answers must be crisp, 3–4 lines, with one key formula/term highlighted.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]

**Define the term "Bio Amplifier". List any four ideal characteristics of a bio amplifier used for ECG signal acquisition.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **bio amplifier** is a precision electronic amplifier that detects and amplifies low-amplitude bioelectric signals (typically 0.1 mV – 5 mV for ECG) from the human body while rejecting noise and interference. It is the **front-end stage** of all biomedical instruments.

**Four ideal characteristics:**
1. **High Gain** ($A_v \geq 1000$) — to lift µV–mV signals to ADC-usable 1–5 V range.
2. **High Input Impedance** ($Z_{in} \geq 10$ MΩ) — to avoid loading the high-impedance electrode–skin interface.
3. **High CMRR** ($\geq 80$ dB) — to reject 50 Hz mains pickup and other common-mode noise.
4. **Low Noise** (input-referred noise < 5 µV rms in 0.05–150 Hz band) — so the signal is not buried in the amplifier's own noise.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**What is the function of the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ in a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier? State the gain equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The resistor $R_G$ is connected **between the inverting inputs of the two input op-amps (A1 and A2)**. It sets the differential gain of the **first stage** of the IA. By varying $R_G$ alone, the user can adjust the overall gain of the bio amplifier **without disturbing the second-stage matching** (and hence without degrading CMRR).

**Gain equation:**
$$
A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}
$$

For $R_F = 47$ kΩ, $R_G = 1$ kΩ, $R_1 = R_2 = 10$ kΩ: $A_v = 95 \times 1 = 95$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Both **Question A** and **Question B** are provided so the student has a genuine internal choice, exactly as per KTU 2024 ESE paper pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier**. Derive the expression for its overall voltage gain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a bio amplifier built using the 3-op-amp IA, $R_F = 47$ kΩ, $R_1 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ, $R_2 = R_4 = 100$ kΩ. (i) Calculate the value of $R_G$ required to set the overall gain to **1000**. (ii) If the four resistors of the difference amplifier each have a 0.1% tolerance, calculate the **CMRR in dB** due to mismatch alone. (iii) If a 1 mV peak ECG signal is applied, what is the output voltage?

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Circuit Operation (3 marks):**
- The IA consists of two input buffer op-amps (A1, A2) and a difference amplifier (A3).
- A1 and A2 form a **symmetric non-inverting pair** with a single gain resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. This guarantees equal common-mode gain in both arms.
- A3 is a **standard difference amplifier** with resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ satisfying the matching condition $R_1/R_3 = R_2/R_4$.
- The output is single-ended, ground-referenced.

**Derivation (4 marks) — write the steps in order:**
1. Virtual short at A1, A2 → node A voltage = $V_1$, node B voltage = $V_2$.
2. Current through $R_G$: $I = (V_1 - V_2)/R_G$.
3. Same current flows through both $R_F$ → $V_{A1} - V_{A2} = (1 + 2R_F/R_G)(V_1 - V_2)$.
4. Difference amplifier gain: $V_{out} = (R_2/R_1)(V_{A1} - V_{A2})$.
5. Total: $V_{out} = (1 + 2R_F/R_G)(R_2/R_1)(V_1 - V_2)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) [3 Marks] — Finding $R_G$ for $A_v = 1000$:**
$$
A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_F}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}
$$
$$
1000 = \left(1 + \frac{2 \cdot 47{,}000}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{100{,}000}{10{,}000}
$$
$$
1000 = \left(1 + \frac{94{,}000}{R_G}\right) \cdot 10
$$
$$
100 = 1 + \frac{94{,}000}{R_G}
$$
$$
\frac{94{,}000}{R_G} = 99
$$
$$
\boxed{\,R_G = \frac{94{,}000}{99} \approx 949.5 \;\Omega\,}
$$
Use the standard value $R_G = 1$ kΩ (close enough; this gives $A_v = 950$).

**(ii) [2 Marks] — CMRR with 0.1% resistor tolerance:**
$$
\text{CMRR} \approx \frac{4}{\delta_1 + \delta_2} = \frac{4}{0.001 + 0.001} = 2000
$$
$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} 2000 \approx 66 \;\text{dB}
$$

**(iii) [2 Marks] — Output for 1 mV ECG input:**
$$
V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = 1000 \cdot 1 \;\text{mV} = 1 \;\text{V (peak)}
$$
If a real ECG QRS of 1 mV peak is amplified by 1000, the output is **1 V peak**, which is easily displayed on the oscilloscope.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **CMRR** of a bio amplifier. A 3-op-amp IA has a differential gain $A_{DM} = 1000$ and a measured common-mode gain $A_{CM} = 0.01$. Calculate the CMRR in dB. Why is high CMRR important in ECG recording?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with diagrams how you would **measure the CMRR and bandwidth** of a bio amplifier in the lab using a function generator and oscilloscope. State the typical bandwidth required for (i) ECG, (ii) EEG, (iii) EMG signals.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) is the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_{DM}$ to the common-mode gain $A_{CM}$ of an amplifier, expressed in decibels:
$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}} \right\vert
$$
It quantifies the amplifier's ability to reject signals that appear *identically* on both inputs (such as 50 Hz mains interference picked up by the body).

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{1000}{0.01} \right\vert = 20 \log_{10} 10^{5} = 20 \times 5 = 100 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Importance in ECG (3 marks):**
- The human body acts as an **antenna** that picks up 50 Hz mains hum and RF interference — these appear equally on both ECG electrodes (true common-mode signals).
- A bio amplifier with **high CMRR** rejects these interference signals while preserving the differential ECG waveform (P-QRS-T).
- Without high CMRR, the 50 Hz noise would **completely swamp** the 1 mV ECG signal, making diagnosis impossible.
- Medical-grade ECG machines require **CMRR > 100 dB** to comply with IEC 60601-1 standards.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**CMRR Measurement Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Set the function generator to a 50 Hz sine wave of 1 V peak-to-peak.
2. **Short the two input terminals together** (apply the same signal to both $V_1$ and $V_2$).
3. Connect the IA output to the oscilloscope (Channel 1) and the function generator output to Channel 2.
4. Measure the *output* peak-to-peak voltage $V_{out,CM}$.
5. Compute: $A_{CM} = V_{out,CM} / V_{in,CM}$.
6. Compute CMRR (dB) = $20 \log_{10} (A_{DM} / A_{CM})$, where $A_{DM}$ is the gain measured separately with a differential input.

**Bandwidth Measurement (2 marks):**
1. Apply a **small differential sine wave** (e.g., 10 mVpp) of mid-band frequency (e.g., 100 Hz) and note the output amplitude — this is the reference level (0 dB).
2. Sweep the function generator frequency from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz.
3. The **lower cutoff $f_L$** is the frequency at which the output drops to 0.707 of reference (–3 dB) on the low side.
4. The **upper cutoff $f_H$** is the frequency at which the output drops to –3 dB on the high side.
5. Bandwidth = $f_H - f_L$.

**Typical Bandwidths (2 marks):**
| Signal | Bandwidth |
|--------|-----------|
| ECG | 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz |
| EEG | 0.5 Hz – 70 Hz |
| EMG | 20 Hz – 1500 Hz |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PCBRL407 viva + record (Board Examiner's Eye-View):**
> 1. **Confusing gain formula** — writing $A_v = 1 + R_F/R_G$ instead of $\mathbf{1 + 2R_F/R_G}$. Remember: there are *two* $R_F$ resistors and *one* $R_G$. The 2× factor is the most-missed mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention input protection diodes + patient isolation** — clinical bio-amps *must* have these; the examiner will explicitly ask in viva.
> 3. **Using 741 op-amp** — fine for academic demo, but the examiner expects justification of why a low-bias, low-noise op-amp (OP07, TL074, AD620) is preferred in biomedical use.
> 4. **Not mentioning the gain–bandwidth trade-off** — increasing $A_v$ shrinks $f_H$ (because GBW is constant). State this in any design question.
> 5. **CMRR expressed without units** — always say **"CMRR = X dB"**, never just the ratio.
> 6. **No mention of safety / patient leakage current** — the IEC 60601-1 limit of **10 µA (Type CF)** is a 2-mark viva trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Quick-Recall — Pin This to Your Wall:**

- **Bio Amplifier = Instrumentation Amplifier (3-op-amp topology)** is the KTU-mandated realization.
- **Five non-negotiables:** High Gain (≥ 1000), High $Z_{in}$ (≥ 10 MΩ), High CMRR (≥ 80 dB), Low Noise, Correct Bandwidth.
- **Total Gain:** $A_v = (1 + 2R_F / R_G) \cdot (R_2 / R_1)$. Gain is set by **a single resistor $R_G$**.
- **First stage** is a current-mode subtractor — inherently common-mode rejecting.
- **Second stage** is a standard difference amplifier — its CMRR depends on $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$ matching.
- **CMRR (dB) due to resistor mismatch:** $\approx 20 \log(4 / (\delta_1 + \delta_2))$.
- **CMRR (dB) in general:** $20 \log \vert A_{DM} / A_{CM} \vert$.
- **Bandwidths:** ECG 0.05–150 Hz · EEG 0.5–70 Hz · EMG 20–1500 Hz.
- **Slew rate** must exceed $2\pi f_{max} V_{peak}$ to avoid distortion of the QRS complex.
- **Safety:** Input protection diodes + isolation barrier + patient leakage current ≤ 10 µA (IEC 60601-1).
- **Lab procedure (record must include):** Circuit diagram, theoretical gain calculation, measured gain, % error, CMRR measurement setup, frequency response table (f vs. gain), and one biomedical signal (ECG/EMG) trace.
- **Commercial ICs (remember for viva):** AD620, INA128, INA333, INA118 — all are 3-op-amp IA chips with CMRR > 100 dB.
- **Real-world note:** The same 3-op-amp IA is the **front-end of every medical device you have ever seen in a hospital** — from a ₹500 pulse oximeter to a ₹50 lakh EEG machine.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module Experiment 2: Bio Amplifier (PCBRL407).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Phase Detector ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **Phase Detector** is an electronic circuit that compares two input signals of nominally the same frequency and produces an output voltage (or current) whose **average (DC) value is a linear function of the phase difference** $\phi$ between the two inputs. In the KTU PCBRL407 (Biomedical Electronics Lab) curriculum, the phase detector is implemented using the **AD630 precision balanced modulator/demodulator** IC, or alternatively using a **CMOS XOR gate (CD4070)** followed by a passive/active low-pass filter. The fundamental transfer characteristic is expressed as: $$V_{out(DC)} = K_d \cdot \phi + V_{offset}$$ where $K_d$ is the **phase detector gain** (in V/radian) and $V_{offset}$ is the DC bias when the phase difference is zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A phase detector is *not* a frequency detector. It operates only when the two inputs are at (or near) the same frequency. If frequencies differ, the output is a beat-frequency waveform, not a stable DC level. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Metronomes" Intuition Imagine two perfectly calibrated metronomes ticking side by side. If you clap only when **both** click at the same instant, the rate of clapping tells you their *frequency match*. But what if you want to know **how late one is compared to the other** (even if both tick at the same speed)? That "lateness" is the **phase difference** $\phi$. A phase detector is the electronic "clap-counter": - **Inputs:** Two sinusoidal (or square) signals $v_1(t)$ and $v_2(t)$ at the *same* frequency $f_0$. - **Output:** A steady DC voltage proportional to the *timing offset* between them. - **Filter:** A low-pass filter that averages out the fast oscillations and leaves only the slow DC component that depends on $\phi$. In biomedical instrumentation, this is invaluable for **lock-in detection** — pulling a tiny physiological signal (e.g., from a photoplethysmography or impedance pneumography probe) out of heavy noise by shifting the detector's reference to match the signal's exact phase. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (AD630 Setup) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Operating Frequency | $f_0$ | **1 kHz to 10 kHz** | Hz | | Supply Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **$\pm 5$ to $\pm 15$** | V | | Phase Detector Gain | $K_d$ | **$\pm 0.5$ to $2.0$** | V/rad | | Linear Range | $\phi_{lin}$ | **$\pm 90°$** | degrees | | Output Offset | $V_{os}$ | **$\leq 1$** | mV | > [!NOTE] > For the XOR-gate phase detector, $K_d$ is normalized to the logic supply rail. For a $5\,\text{V}$ CMOS XOR, $K_d \approx 1.59\,\text{V/rad}$ (i.e., $V_{CC}/\pi$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Flow The AD630-based phase detector operates on the principle of **synchronous demodulation**: 1. **Reference signal ($v_{ref}$):** A square wave (typically derived from the master oscillator) drives the AD630's internal channel-select switch. This toggles the polarity of the signal path at the reference frequency. 2. **Input signal ($v_{in}$):** A sinusoid (often containing noise + the bioelectric signal) enters the AD630. 3. **Multiplication (Chopper action):** The AD630 multiplies $v_{in}$ by $+1$ or $-1$ depending on the instantaneous state of $v_{ref}$. This is mathematically equivalent to multiplying by a square wave $sq(t)$ whose fundamental Fourier component is a cosine at the reference frequency. 4. **Synchronous demodulation:** After multiplication, the original signal's spectrum is shifted: the component that was in-phase with $v_{ref}$ collapses to **DC**, while noise and out-of-phase components remain at higher frequencies. 5. **Low-pass filtering:** An RC low-pass filter at the output averages the chopped signal, yielding a DC voltage: $$V_{out} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot V_{in(peak)} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$ where $\phi$ is the phase difference between $v_{in}$ and $v_{ref}$. 6. **Phase-to-voltage conversion:** By introducing a calibrated phase shift (using an all-pass filter or RC network) between $v_{in}$ and $v_{ref}$, one can characterize $V_{out}$ vs. $\phi$ — this is the **transfer characteristic** required in the KTU lab record. ### 2.2 Why the AD630? (Engineering Justification) The AD630 is preferred over discrete multiplier ICs (e.g., AD633) for biomedical labs because of: - **High common-mode rejection** (>100 dB at DC) — essential when bioelectric signals ride on large electrode offset voltages. - **Pin-selectable gain** of $\pm 1$ or $\pm 2$. - **Wide dynamic range** with low distortion. - **Channel-select inputs** that accept CMOS/TTL square waves directly — no external level-shifting required. ### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Multiplication When two sinusoids at the same frequency $f_0$ with phase difference $\phi$ are multiplied and low-pass filtered, the trigonometric product-to-sum identity collapses the high-frequency $2f_0$ term to zero, leaving only the DC component proportional to $\cos(\phi)$: $$v_{in}(t) \cdot v_{ref}(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_0 t) \cdot B\sin(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$$ $$= \frac{AB}{2}\cos(\phi) - \frac{AB}{2}\cos(4\pi f_0 t + \phi)$$ The LPF removes the $2f_0$ term, leaving $V_{out} = \frac{AB}{2}\cos(\phi)$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos(\phi)$ | AD630 output for square-wave ref × sine input | V | | 2 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\pi}\left(\pi - 2\phi\right)$ | XOR phase detector (rising-edge convention) | V | | 3 | $K_d = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{d\phi} = -\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\sin(\phi_0)$ | Phase detector gain (slope at operating point) | V/rad | | 4 | $\phi_{linear} = \pm 90°$ | Range over which AD630 output is monotonic | degrees | | 5 | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ | Low-pass filter cutoff for output averaging | Hz | | 6 | $\text{SNR}_{out} = \text{SNR}_{in} + 10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{BW_{in}}{BW_{out}}\right)$ | Lock-in detection SNR enhancement | dB | | 7 | $V_{os} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot V_{ref,off}$ | Output offset due to reference channel offset | V | > [!NOTE] > In all rows above, the phase $\phi$ is expressed in **radians** in the formulas but the KTU lab manual typically uses **degrees** in plots. Always convert: $\phi_{rad} = \phi_{deg} \times \pi / 180$. ### 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Utility Phase detectors underpin several clinically critical instruments: - **Phase-locked loop (PLL) heart-rate monitors** — lock onto the R-wave of the ECG despite motion artifact. - **Impedance pneumography** — the chest-wall impedance is excited with a high-frequency carrier, and the phase-sensitive detector extracts the respiration-modulated component. - **Laser Doppler flowmetry** — extracts the Doppler-shifted signal from a noisy optical backscatter. - **Lock-in amplifiers** (e.g., SR830) — the gold-standard tool for recovering sub-$\mu$V signals in millivolt-scale noise, used in evoked-potential audiometry and electroretinography. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — AD630 Transfer Characteristic **Given:** Input signal $v_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and reference square wave $v_{ref}(t)$ at the same fundamental frequency with phase lag $\phi$. **Step 1 — Expand the reference square wave in its Fourier series:** A 50% duty-cycle square wave with phase shift $\phi$ can be written as: $$v_{ref}(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin\!\left(n\omega t + n\phi\right)$$ **Step 2 — Multiply the input by the reference (chopper action):** $$v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(t) \cdot v_{ref}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t + n\phi)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the product-to-sum identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$ to the $n=1$ fundamental term:** For $n = 1$: $$V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \sin(\omega t + \phi) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t + \phi)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Identify the DC component vs. the AC component:** - **DC term:** $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(\phi)$ — this is what we want. - **AC term:** $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(2\omega t + \phi)$ — oscillates at $2\omega$, will be removed by the LPF. Higher harmonics ($n = 3, 5, \ldots$) produce terms at $2\omega, 4\omega, 6\omega, \ldots$ which are also rejected by the LPF. **Step 5 — Output after the low-pass filter:** $$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(\phi)}$$ **Step 6 — Sensitivity (gain) calculation:** Differentiating with respect to $\phi$: $$K_d(\phi) = \frac{dV_{out}}{d\phi} = -\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\sin(\phi)$$ The **maximum sensitivity** occurs at $\phi = 90°$ (i.e., when the two signals are in quadrature): $$K_{d,max} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\quad [\text{V/rad}]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical example (KTU lab typical):** If $V_m = 5\,\text{V}$ and $\phi = 60°$, then $V_{out} = (2 \times 5 / \pi) \times \cos(60°) = 3.183 \times 0.5 = 1.59\,\text{V}$. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — XOR Phase Detector Transfer Characteristic **Given:** Two square waves $A$ and $B$ at the same frequency, XOR'ed, then averaged. **Step 1 — When $\phi = 0°$ (in phase):** XOR output is *always* LOW $\Rightarrow$ average = 0 V. **Step 2 — When $\phi = 90°$ (in quadrature):** XOR output is HIGH for exactly 50% of the period $\Rightarrow$ average = $V_{CC}/2$. **Step 3 — When $\phi = 180°$ (anti-phase):** XOR output is *always* HIGH $\Rightarrow$ average = $V_{CC}$. **Step 4 — Linear interpolation for general $0 \le \phi \le \pi$:** The duty cycle of the XOR output is $\phi/\pi$, so: $$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\phi}{\pi}$$ **Step 5 — For phase shifts beyond $\pi$ (or negative phase):** The XOR output duty cycle decreases again, giving a sawtooth transfer characteristic. The detector is **monotonic** over $\phi \in [0, \pi]$ and repeats with period $2\pi$. $$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\phi}{\pi}, \quad 0 \le \phi \le \pi}$$ **Step 6 — Phase detector gain:** $$K_d = \frac{dV_{out}}{d\phi} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi}$$ For $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$: $K_d = 5/\pi \approx 1.59\,\text{V/rad}$. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Topology | Criterion | AD630-Based | XOR-Gate (CD4070) | Analog Multiplier (AD633) | |---|---|---|---| | Input signal type | Sine or square | Square only | Sine or square | | Linearity of $\phi \to V$ | Excellent ($\cos$) | Piecewise-linear (sawtooth) | Excellent ($\cos$) | | Sensitivity to amplitude | Requires stable $V_m$ | Insensitive (digital) | Requires stable $V_m$ | | Noise rejection (Q-factor) | High | Moderate | High | | Cost | Medium (~$5) | Low (~$0.50) | Medium (~$8) | | Preferred for biomedical? | **Yes (lock-in)** | Yes (PLL, digital) | Yes (research) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — AD630 Phase Detector Experiment ```mermaid flowchart LR fnc["Function Generator 1
f1 1kHz sine"]:::src phs["Phase Shifter
RC All Pass 0 to 180"]:::proc fnr["Function Generator 2
f2 1kHz square ref"]:::src ad6["AD630
Balanced Modulator
Pin 1 INA Pin 14 INB
Pin 13 SELA Pin 12 SELB"]:::core lpf["Active LPF
Rf 10k Cf 0.1uF
fc approx 159 Hz"]:::proc dvm["DMM / CRO
DC coupled"]:::out sw1(["Switch SW1
Select phase 0 45 90 135 180"]):::ui sw2(["Switch SW2
Gain plus or minus 1 or 2"]):::ui fnc -- v_in sine --> phs phs -- v_in shifted --> ad6 fnr -- v_ref square --> ad6 ad6 --> lpf lpf --> dvm sw1 -. config .-> phs sw2 -. config .-> ad6 classDef src fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#0d47a1 classDef proc fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,color:#e65100 classDef core fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c classDef out fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 classDef ui fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#880e4f ``` ### 4.2 Signal Flow — Synchronous Demodulation Principle ```mermaid flowchart TB a["v_in t = Vm sin omega t
Signal plus Noise"]:::sig b["v_ref t = sq omega t plus phi
Square wave reference"]:::sig c["Multiplier
Chopper"]:::core d["LPF
Removes 2f components"]:::proc e["Vout DC = 2Vm by pi cos phi"]:::out a --> c b --> c c -- chopped waveform spectrum shifted to DC plus 2f --> d d -- DC retained 2f rejected --> e classDef sig fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#01579b classDef core fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c classDef proc fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,color:#e65100 classDef out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.3 Expected Transfer Characteristic (Plot Reference) ```mermaid xychart-beta title "AD630 Phase Detector: Vout vs Phase Difference" x-axis "Phase phi in degrees" [-90, -60, -30, 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180] y-axis "Vout in Volts" 0 --> 3.2 line [3.18, 2.76, 1.59, 0.00, -1.59, -2.76, -3.18, -2.76, -1.59, 0.00] ``` > [!NOTE] > The plot above uses $V_m = 5\,\text{V}$ and the relation $V_{out} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos(\phi)$. Note that the curve is **monotonically decreasing** from $+90°$ to $-90°$, with maximum sensitivity (steepest slope) at $\phi = 0°$ where $K_d = 2V_m/\pi \approx 3.18\,\text{V/rad}$. The $y$-intercept at $\phi=0$ is the **in-phase DC value**, used in the lab to calibrate the detector's null. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a phase detector. Mention its key transfer characteristic.** **Model Answer:** A phase detector is a circuit that compares two signals of the same frequency and produces an output voltage proportional to the **phase difference $\phi$** between them. The key transfer characteristic is: $$V_{out} = K_d \cdot \phi + V_{offset}$$ For an AD630-based detector, the output follows $V_{out} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos(\phi)$, which is monotonically decreasing over $\phi \in [-90°, +90°]$ with maximum sensitivity $K_d = 2V_m/\pi$ at $\phi = 0°$. *(3 marks)* #### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List two biomedical applications of a phase detector.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Lock-in amplification** in photoplethysmography (PPG) to recover pulsatile blood-volume signals buried in ambient-light noise. 2. **Phase-sensitive detection in impedance pneumography** for respiration-rate monitoring, where the chest-wall impedance modulates a high-frequency carrier's amplitude and phase. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an **AD630-based phase detector**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An AD630 phase detector is driven by a $5\,\text{V}$ peak sine input and a square reference at $1\,\text{kHz}$. The phase shift between the two is varied from $0°$ to $180°$ in steps of $30°$. Calculate the expected DC output voltage at each step. Plot the transfer characteristic. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution for (a) The block diagram consists of: **Function Generator → Phase Shifter → AD630 → LPF → DMM/CRO**. **Operation:** The input sine wave $v_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ enters the AD630's signal channel. The reference square wave $v_{ref}(t)$ drives the channel-select pins (SELA, SELB) of the AD630, which internally functions as a precision chopper that multiplies $v_{in}$ by $+1$ or $-1$ depending on the state of $v_{ref}$. The product contains a DC term proportional to $\cos(\phi)$ and an AC term at $2\omega$. The LPF (RC network with $f_c \ll 2f_0$) rejects the AC term and passes the DC component. **Derivation:** $$v_{in}(t) \cdot v_{ref}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t + n\phi)$$ For the fundamental $n=1$: $$= \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(\phi) - \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(2\omega t + \phi)$$ After the LPF: $$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(\phi) \quad \text{volts}}$$ ##### Model Solution for (b) Using $V_m = 5\,\text{V}$, the constant $\frac{2V_m}{\pi} = \frac{10}{\pi} = 3.183\,\text{V}$. | Phase $\phi$ (deg) | $\cos(\phi)$ | $V_{out} = 3.183 \cdot \cos(\phi)$ (V) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 1.000 | **+3.183** | | 30 | 0.866 | **+2.757** | | 60 | 0.500 | **+1.592** | | 90 | 0.000 | **0.000** | | 120 | −0.500 | **−1.592** | | 150 | −0.866 | **−2.757** | | 180 | −1.000 | **−3.183** | The transfer characteristic is a **monotonically decreasing cosine curve**, crossing zero at $\phi = 90°$ (the **quadrature point**). Plot: $V_{out}$ on the y-axis (vertical, in V, range ±3.5 V), $\phi$ on the x-axis (horizontal, in degrees, range 0° to 180°). Mark all seven calculated points and connect with a smooth cosine curve. --- #### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the working of an **XOR-gate phase detector** using the CD4070 IC. Derive the expression for its output voltage. *(7 marks)* **(b)** In an XOR phase detector operating from a $5\,\text{V}$ supply, what is the output DC voltage when the two square-wave inputs are (i) in phase, (ii) at $45°$ phase shift, (iii) in quadrature, (iv) at $135°$ phase shift, (v) in anti-phase? Compute the phase detector gain $K_d$. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution for (a) The CD4070 contains four CMOS XOR gates. Two square waves $A$ and $B$ at the same frequency but with phase difference $\phi$ are fed into one XOR gate. The XOR output is HIGH only when the inputs differ. - **Duty cycle** of XOR output = $\phi / \pi$ (for $0 \le \phi \le \pi$). - **Average (DC) output** = $V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{\phi}{\pi}$. This is followed by an RC low-pass filter (typically $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ giving $f_c \approx 159\,\text{Hz}$, much less than the input frequency of $1\,\text{kHz}$), which averages the XOR waveform to produce the DC output voltage. **Derivation:** $$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\phi}{\pi}, \quad 0 \le \phi \le \pi$$ The transfer characteristic is a **sawtooth waveform** that rises linearly from $0$ V to $V_{CC}$ as $\phi$ goes from $0$ to $\pi$, then resets to $0$ for $\phi$ going from $\pi$ to $2\pi$. The detector is monotonic only over $[0, \pi]$, which gives a linear range of $\pi$ rad (or $180°$). ##### Model Solution for (b) Using $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $V_{out} = 5\phi/\pi$: | Case | Phase $\phi$ | $V_{out} = 5\phi/\pi$ (V) | |---|---|---| | (i) In phase | $0° = 0$ rad | **0.000 V** | | (ii) 45° shift | $45° = \pi/4$ rad | **1.250 V** | | (iii) Quadrature | $90° = \pi/2$ rad | **2.500 V** | | (iv) 135° shift | $135° = 3\pi/4$ rad | **3.750 V** | | (v) Anti-phase | $180° = \pi$ rad | **5.000 V** | **Phase detector gain:** $$K_d = \frac{dV_{out}}{d\phi} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi} = \frac{5}{\pi} \approx \mathbf{1.59\,\text{V/rad}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing the two formulas:** AD630 gives a **cosine** characteristic (monotonic over ±90°); XOR gives a **linear** sawtooth (monotonic over 0–180°). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly. > 2. **Forgetting the filter:** Many students derive the multiplied signal but forget to write the LPF step. Always state that the LPF removes the $2\omega$ term. > 3. **Units mismatch:** $K_d$ must be in V/**rad** for lock-in detector analysis. If you write V/degree, you must convert by multiplying by $180/\pi$ in subsequent PLL equations. > 4. **No amplitude normalization:** The cosine formula assumes the *peak* amplitude $V_m$, not peak-to-peak. KTU examiners explicitly check this. > 5. **Skipping the Fourier series of the square wave:** Writing $V_{out} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\phi$ without showing the series expansion for the square wave loses the methodology marks. ### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist - **Definition:** A phase detector outputs a DC voltage proportional to the *phase difference* $\phi$ between two same-frequency signals. - **AD630 Transfer:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos(\phi)$; **monotonic** over $\phi \in [-90°, +90°]$. - **XOR Transfer:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\pi}\phi$; **monotonic** over $\phi \in [0°, 180°]$ (sawtooth). - **Block diagram:** Function Gen → Phase Shifter → Phase Detector IC → LPF → DMM. - **Filter cut-off:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f) \ll 2f_0$ (typically $f_c \approx f_0/10$ to $f_0/5$). - **Sensitivity:** $K_d = -2V_m/\pi \cdot \sin(\phi)$ for AD630; $K_d = V_{CC}/\pi$ (constant) for XOR. - **Quadrature point ($\phi = 90°$):** $V_{out} = 0$ for AD630; $V_{out} = V_{CC}/2$ for XOR. - **Anti-phase ($\phi = 180°$):** $V_{out} = -2V_m/\pi$ for AD630; $V_{out} = V_{CC}$ for XOR. - **Biomedical apps:** Lock-in PPG, impedance pneumography, evoked-potential detection, heart-rate PLL. - **KTU Examiner Loves:** Drawing the transfer characteristic with the operating point marked, and showing the LPF step in the derivation. - **Common ICs:** AD630 (precision), CD4070 (XOR), CD4046 (PLL with on-chip PD), AD633 (analog multiplier). - **Lab Tip:** Always null the offset with $\phi = 90°$ (AD630) or $\phi = 0°$ (XOR) before taking readings.

topics.title: Active Filters - Notch, HPF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Active Filters - Notch, HPF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Active Filters — Formal Definition

An **active filter** is an analog frequency-selective circuit that uses one or more **active components** (typically an **operational amplifier**) along with passive components (resistors and capacitors) to pass or reject specific frequency bands of an input signal. Unlike passive filters, active filters provide **gain**, **buffering**, and **isolation** in addition to frequency selectivity, and they do not require bulky inductors.

In the context of **biomedical electronics**, active filters are indispensable for:
- Removing **50 Hz / 60 Hz power-line interference** (Notch filter) from ECG, EMG, and EEG signals.
- Eliminating **baseline wander** and DC offsets (HPF) in biopotential recordings.
- Rejecting **high-frequency noise** above the signal bandwidth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PCBRL407):** The experiment deals with the **design, construction, and frequency-response verification** of a **Notch filter (50 Hz)** and a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** using an op-amp (typically **µA741**). Students are expected to plot the **gain vs. frequency response** on a **semi-log graph sheet** and determine the **−3 dB cutoff frequency** and the **notch frequency (f₀)**.

---

## 1.2 The Two Filters at a Glance

| Filter | Full Name | Function | Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HPF** | High-Pass Filter | Passes frequencies **above** a cutoff; attenuates **low frequencies** and **DC** | Remove **DC offset**, **baseline drift**, **motion artifacts** |
| **Notch (BSF)** | Band-Stop / Band-Reject Filter | Rejects a **narrow band** of frequencies; passes all others | Remove **50 Hz mains hum** from ECG/EMG/EEG |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club" 🏢

Imagine a nightclub with two bouncers working in series at the entrance:

- **HPF Bouncer (Mr. High-Pass):** "If you are too *low* and *slow* (low frequencies or DC), I will not let you in. Only *fast, energetic* signals (high frequencies) pass through." This is your **High-Pass Filter**.
- **Notch Bouncer (Mr. Notch):** "Everyone is welcome… **EXCEPT** people from a *specific troublesome group* (e.g., exactly 50 Hz) — they get blocked at a narrow door." This is your **Notch Filter**.

Together, they clean up a noisy signal: the HPF removes the lazy DC drift, and the Notch removes the exact frequency of mains interference.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must Remember)

- **Cutoff Frequency (f_c)** — frequency at which output power is halved (**−3 dB point**).
- **Notch Frequency (f₀)** — exact center frequency being rejected by the notch filter.
- **Roll-off Rate** — how steeply gain falls outside the passband. **First-order: 20 dB/decade; second-order: 40 dB/decade.**
- **Quality Factor (Q)** — selectivity of the filter; higher Q means narrower rejection band.
- **Passband Gain (A₀)** — mid-band gain of the active filter (set by op-amp feedback).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constant:** The Indian **mains supply frequency is 50 Hz** (in the US it is 60 Hz). Biomedical notch filters in India are almost always tuned to **f₀ = 50 Hz** to remove power-line interference picked up by the body and electrodes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order Active High-Pass Filter (HPF)

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology

A first-order active HPF is built by placing a **series capacitor (C)** at the input of a non-inverting or inverting op-amp stage, with a **resistor (R)** to ground forming the feedback network.

For a **non-inverting HPF**:
- The **passive CR network** at the input behaves as a high-pass.
- The **op-amp** provides buffering and a fixed passband gain: $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$.

### 2.1.2 Transfer Function (How It Works)

The passive CR network alone gives:
$$H_{passive}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The op-amp multiplies this by the closed-loop gain $A_v$. The complete transfer function is:
$$\boxed{\,H(j\omega) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}\,}$$

### 2.1.3 Operational Logic

1. At **low frequencies** ($\omega \to 0$): capacitor acts as an **open circuit** → no signal passes → output ≈ 0.
2. At **high frequencies** ($\omega \to \infty$): capacitor acts as a **short circuit** → full signal passes → output ≈ $A_v \cdot V_{in}$.
3. At **cutoff frequency** $f_c$: gain drops to $A_v / \sqrt{2}$, i.e., **−3 dB**.

---

## 2.2 Second-Order Sallen-Key High-Pass Filter

For sharper roll-off, KTU labs typically use a **second-order Sallen-Key HPF**. It uses **two capacitors and two resistors** with a **unity-gain op-amp follower** (or non-inverting amplifier).

General second-order HPF transfer function:
$$H(s) = A_0 \cdot \frac{s^2}{s^2 + \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}$$

For a **unity-gain Sallen-Key HPF** (equal components: $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

| Parameter | Equation |
|---|---|
| **Cutoff frequency** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Quality factor** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3 - A_v}$ (Butterworth when $A_v = 1.586$) |
| **Roll-off** | 40 dB/decade (12 dB/octave) |

---

## 2.3 Twin-T Notch Filter (Active Band-Stop)

### 2.3.1 Why "Twin-T"?

The circuit is built from **two parallel T-networks**:
- **Low-pass T-network** (two series Rs and a shunt C) — passes low frequencies.
- **High-pass T-network** (two series Cs and a shunt R) — passes high frequencies.

The two networks have outputs that **sum to unity** at all frequencies **EXCEPT** at $f_0$, where they cancel each other, creating a sharp notch.

### 2.3.2 Notch Frequency

For equal components ($R_1 = R_2 = 2R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C/2$ for symmetry, or simply $R$ and $C$ for the equal-component case):
$$\boxed{\,f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The notch frequency of a Twin-T filter is the **same formula** as the cutoff of a single RC HPF/LPF — students often confuse this. The notch is at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, not at $1/(4\pi RC)$.

### 2.3.3 Why Add an Op-Amp? (The "Active" Part)

A passive Twin-T notch has a **Q factor of only 0.25**, giving a very **wide and shallow** notch. By buffering with an op-amp and using **positive feedback**, the **Q is boosted to typically 5–50**, producing a **sharp, deep notch** at exactly 50 Hz.

Active Twin-T (with op-amp in feedback) Q-factor:
$$Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - A_v)}$$

where $A_v$ is the gain of the feedback amplifier (must be slightly **less than 1** for stability, typically $A_v \approx 0.95$–$0.99$).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars (\vert) used in tables** to avoid breaking Markdown rendering. The notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ is replaced with $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or contextually described.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1st-order HPF cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | −3 dB point |
| 2 | 1st-order HPF gain (mag) | $\lvert H(j\omega) \rvert = \dfrac{A_v \, \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | — | Magnitude response |
| 3 | 2nd-order Sallen-Key HPF $f_c$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Hz | For equal R, C: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| 4 | 2nd-order HPF Q-factor | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}{R_1 C_2 + R_2 C_1 + (1-A_v)R_1 C_1}$ | — | Butterworth: $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| 5 | Twin-T Notch frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | 50 Hz for biomedical use |
| 6 | Notch Q (passive) | $Q = 0.25$ | — | Very low — wide notch |
| 7 | Notch Q (active) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{4(1 - A_v)}$ | — | Tune $A_v$ for desired sharpness |
| 8 | −3 dB condition | $\lvert H(f_c) \rvert = \dfrac{A_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | — | Half-power point |
| 9 | Roll-off (1st order) | 20 dB/decade | — | 6 dB/octave |
| 10 | Roll-off (2nd order) | 40 dB/decade | — | 12 dB/octave |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG machines (e.g., Philips PageWriter):** use a 50 Hz notch as the very **first stage** after the instrumentation amplifier.
- **EEG recording systems:** cascaded HPF (0.5 Hz) + LPF (40 Hz) + Notch (50 Hz) form the standard **biomedical signal conditioning chain**.
- **Pacemaker sense amplifiers:** use HPFs to reject T-wave and DC offset, ensuring only QRS complexes are detected.
- **EMG equipment:** notch filters remove radiated RF and mains hum before the signal is digitized.
- **Defibrillators / patient monitors:** notch prevents mains interference from triggering false alarms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Active HPF Transfer Function

### Circuit Description
A non-inverting op-amp has at its input a **voltage divider** formed by capacitor $C$ (series) and resistor $R$ (to ground). The op-amp output is $V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_+$, where $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_i$.

### Step 1: Voltage at the Non-Inverting Input
The input is $V_{in}$, applied across the series combination of $C$ and $R$. Using the voltage divider rule in the **frequency domain** (impedance of $C$ is $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$):

$$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

### Step 2: Simplify the Expression
Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{j\omega RC + 1}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Non-Inverting Op-Amp Gain
The op-amp multiplies $V_+$ by the closed-loop gain $A_v$:

$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_+ = A_v \cdot V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 4: Final Transfer Function

$$\boxed{\,\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_v \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}\,}$$

### Step 5: Magnitude Response

$$\lvert H(j\omega) \rvert = A_v \cdot \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

### Step 6: Phase Response

$$\angle H(j\omega) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

### Step 7: Cutoff Frequency Identification
At cutoff, $\omega RC = 1$:

$$2\pi f_c R C = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Twin-T Notch Filter Transfer Function (Passive Form)

### Step 1: Label the Two T-Networks
- **T1 (low-pass arm):** two series resistors $R$ and a shunt capacitor $2C$ (commonly, the "2C" formulation keeps the math symmetric).
- **T2 (high-pass arm):** two series capacitors $C$ and a shunt resistor $R/2$.

### Step 2: Compute the Output of Each Arm at the Junction

**Low-pass arm (T1) output** $V_{LP}$ at the central node (analysis using superposition on the symmetrical T-network):

$$V_{LP} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

(approximation valid for symmetric design).

**High-pass arm (T2) output** $V_{HP}$ at the central node:

$$V_{HP} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 3: Sum the Two Arms
At the node connecting both T-networks (the notch output):

$$V_{out} = V_{LP} + V_{HP} = V_{in} \left[\frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} + \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}\right]$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC} = V_{in}$$

Wait — this is the trivial case. The notch arises because the **two T-networks are NOT simply summed**, but feed into a common node with cross-coupling. The full transfer function (with both arms contributing currents into the node) is:

$$H(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + 4 s \omega_0 + \omega_0^2}$$

where $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$.

### Step 5: Identify the Notch Frequency
The numerator $s^2 + \omega_0^2 = 0$ when $s = \pm j\omega_0$, i.e., at frequency $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$.

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

### Step 6: Identify the Q-Factor
Comparing the denominator to the standard second-order form $s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2$:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = 4\omega_0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{Q = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25}$$

This confirms the **low Q** of the passive Twin-T — and explains why an **op-amp with positive feedback** is added to enhance Q.

---

## 3.3 Design Example: 50 Hz Notch Filter (KTU Lab Standard)

**Given:** Design a Twin-T notch filter to remove 50 Hz interference. Choose $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$.

**Step 1:** Apply $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ and solve for $R$:

$$50 = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot R \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} = 31{,}831 \, \Omega \approx 31.8 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 2:** For symmetric Twin-T, use:
- $R_1 = R_2 = 2R = 63.6 \, k\Omega$ (or use $R = 31.8 \, k\Omega$ with $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$).
- $C_1 = C_2 = C = 0.1 \, \mu F$, $2C = 0.2 \, \mu F$, $C/2 = 0.05 \, \mu F$.

**Step 3:** For active Q-enhancement, set feedback gain $A_v = 0.95$ (so $1 - A_v = 0.05$):

$$Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - 0.95)} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5$$

A Q of 5 gives a **notch bandwidth** of:
$$BW = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \, Hz$$

So the filter **strongly rejects 45–55 Hz** and passes everything else.

---

## 3.4 Design Example: 1st-Order HPF with $f_c$ = 0.5 Hz (ECG Baseline Removal)

**Given:** ECG HPF with $f_c = 0.5$ Hz, $C = 1 \, \mu F$.

**Step 1:** Solve for R:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} = 318{,}309 \, \Omega \approx 318 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Choose passband gain $A_v = 2$ (so $R_f = R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$ for non-inverting amp).

**Step 3:** Verify roll-off:
- At 0.5 Hz: gain is $A_v / \sqrt{2} = 1.414$ (−3 dB).
- At 0.05 Hz (decade below): gain drops by 20 dB → $A_v = 0.2$.
- At 0.005 Hz: gain drops another 20 dB → $A_v = 0.02$.

This confirms the standard **20 dB/decade** roll-off of a 1st-order filter.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Specification | 1st-Order HPF | 2nd-Order Sallen-Key HPF | Twin-T Notch |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Components** | 1R, 1C + op-amp | 2R, 2C + op-amp | 4R, 3C + op-amp |
| **Roll-off** | 20 dB/decade | 40 dB/decade | Very sharp at $f_0$ |
| **Q-factor** | 0.707 | Tunable (Butterworth = 0.707) | 0.25 (passive), 5–50 (active) |
| **Cutoff / Notch** | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| **Biomedical use** | Baseline drift | Sharper DC removal | 50 Hz line hum |
| **KTU 2024 lab use** | Yes (common) | Optional (advanced) | Yes (mandatory) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Active HPF Signal Flow

<!-- HTML comment removed: anchor structure required -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Vin: Input Signal with DC Drift] --> nodeC[Series Capacitor C]
    nodeC --> nodeR[Shunt Resistor R to Ground]
    nodeR --> nodeOp[Non-Inverting Op-Amp UA741]
    nodeOp --> nodeGain[Passband Gain Av equals 1 plus Rf divided by Ri]
    nodeGain --> nodeOut[Vout: DC Blocked Frequencies Above Fc Passed]
    nodeFb[Feedback Network Rf and Ri] -.-> nodeOp
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from $V_{in}$ → series $C$ → node $V_+$ → op-amp → $V_{out}$. The capacitor blocks DC and low frequencies; the op-amp provides gain and isolation.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Twin-T Active Notch Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIn[Vin: ECG with 50 Hz Mains Hum] --> nodeSplitter{Signal Splitter}
    nodeSplitter --> nodeLP[Low Pass T Network: R, R, and 2C]
    nodeSplitter --> nodeHP[High Pass T Network: C, C, and R over 2]
    nodeLP --> nodeSum[Virtual Summing Node]
    nodeHP --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeOp[Op-Amp UA741 with Positive Feedback]
    nodeOp --> nodeFb[Feedback Resistor Rf for Q Enhancement]
    nodeFb -.-> nodeOp
    nodeOp --> nodeOut[Vout: 50 Hz Rejected All Other Frequencies Passed]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The input is split into two T-networks — one passing lows, one passing highs. The op-amp with positive feedback boosts Q, producing a **sharp notch exactly at 50 Hz**.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Biomedical Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeElec[Biopotential Electrodes on Patient Body] --> nodeIA[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    nodeIA --> nodeHPF[First Stage HPF fc equals 0.5 Hz Removes DC Drift]
    nodeHPF --> nodeNotch[Second Stage Notch Filter f0 equals 50 Hz Removes Mains Hum]
    nodeNotch --> nodeLPF[Third Stage LPF fc equals 40 Hz Removes High Freq Noise]
    nodeLPF --> nodeADC[ADC and Microcontroller]
    nodeADC --> nodeDisp[Display: ECG Waveform Clean]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **standard biomedical signal conditioning chain** used in ECG/EMG/EEG. The HPF and Notch (the focus of KTU Experiment 2) are the **first and second stages**, ensuring a clean signal before digitization.

---

## 4.4 Frequency Response Curves (Schematic Description)

### HPF Response (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFL[Flat Stopband Below fc: Gain Approaches 0 dB] --> nodeAx[Horizontal Axis: Log Frequency in Hz]
    nodeAx --> nodeCurve[Gain Drops at 20 dB per Decade Above fc]
    nodeCurve --> nodeOut2[Passband Above fc with Gain Av]
```

| Region | Frequency Range | Gain Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Stopband** | $f \ll f_c$ | Attenuates at −20 dB/decade (1st order) |
| **Transition** | $f = f_c$ | Gain = $A_v / \sqrt{2}$ (−3 dB point) |
| **Passband** | $f \gg f_c$ | Gain ≈ $A_v$ (flat) |

### Notch Response (Conceptual Sketch)

| Region | Frequency Range | Gain Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Passband (low side)** | $f \ll f_0$ | Gain ≈ 1 (0 dB) |
| **Notch** | $f = f_0$ | Gain drops to ≈ 0 (−∞ dB ideally, −30 to −50 dB practical) |
| **Passband (high side)** | $f \gg f_0$ | Gain ≈ 1 (0 dB) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define an active filter. Why are active filters preferred over passive filters in biomedical instrumentation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **active filter** is a frequency-selective circuit that uses an **active device (op-amp)** along with **passive components (R, C)** to pass or reject specific frequency bands, while providing **gain and buffering**.

Active filters are preferred in biomedical instrumentation because:

1. **Gain is provided** by the op-amp — passive filters always have gain ≤ 1 (insertion loss).
2. **No inductors required** — biomedical signals are low-frequency (0.1 Hz to 1 kHz), where inductors would be impractically large. Capacitors are smaller, cheaper, and more accurate.
3. **High input impedance and low output impedance** — prevents loading of the high-impedance biopotential electrode source (typically 10–50 kΩ).
4. **Isolation between stages** — cascading stages does not affect the Q-factor or tuning.
5. **Easier tuning of Q and gain** — set precisely by resistor ratios.

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the 5 advantages — list any 4.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is a notch filter? Why is a 50 Hz notch filter used in ECG recording?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **notch filter** (also called **band-stop** or **band-reject filter**) is a circuit that **rejects a very narrow band of frequencies** while passing all frequencies above and below that band.

A **50 Hz notch filter** is used in ECG recording because:

1. The **mains power supply in India operates at 50 Hz** (US: 60 Hz).
2. The patient's body and the ECG cables act as **antennas**, picking up **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** from nearby power lines, fluorescent lamps, and equipment.
3. This 50 Hz interference **overlaps with the ECG signal bandwidth** (0.5–40 Hz) and cannot be removed by a simple LPF.
4. The notch filter **removes exactly 50 Hz** without distorting the rest of the ECG waveform (P, QRS, T).
5. It is typically placed **after the instrumentation amplifier** and before the ADC in the ECG signal chain.

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for notch definition, 1 mark for "50 Hz = Indian mains", 1 mark for biomedical justification.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

#### **(a) Design a first-order active High-Pass Filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 1$ kHz. Assume $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$ and op-amp $\mu A741$ with passband gain of 2. Draw the circuit and derive the transfer function.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose capacitor and calculate resistor:**

Given $f_c = 1000$ Hz, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F = 10^{-8}$ F.

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1000 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15{,}915 \, \Omega \approx 15.9 \, k\Omega$$

Use standard value $R = 15.9 \, k\Omega$ (or $16 \, k\Omega$).

**Step 2 — Set passband gain $A_v = 2$:**

For non-inverting op-amp: $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_i = 2$ → $R_f = R_i$. Choose $R_f = R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$.

**Step 3 — Transfer function derivation:**

The voltage at the non-inverting input is:

$$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Op-amp output:

$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_+ = 2 \cdot V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

$$\boxed{H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{2 \, j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

Substituting $RC = 1/(2\pi f_c) = 1/(2\pi \times 1000) = 1.59 \times 10^{-4}$ s:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega / (2\pi \times 1000)}{1 + j\omega / (2\pi \times 1000)}$$

**Step 4 — Verification at key frequencies:**

- At $f = 10$ Hz (decade below $f_c$): $\lvert H \rvert = 2 \cdot 0.1 / \sqrt{1.01} \approx 0.2$ (−20 dB).
- At $f = 1000$ Hz = $f_c$: $\lvert H \rvert = 2 / \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (−3 dB).
- At $f = 10$ kHz (decade above $f_c$): $\lvert H \rvert \approx 2$ (0 dB, full passband).

This confirms the **20 dB/decade** roll-off of a first-order HPF.

**Step 5 — Circuit diagram (text representation):**

> Series $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$ → non-inverting input of $\mu A741$ (pins 3) → output (pin 6) → $R = 15.9 \, k\Omega$ from $V_+$ node to ground. Op-amp feedback: $R_f = 10 \, k\Omega$ from pin 6 to pin 2, $R_i = 10 \, k\Omega$ from pin 2 to ground. Pin 7 to +12 V, pin 4 to −12 V.

---

#### **(b) With the HPF designed in part (a), an input signal of 100 mV peak-to-peak at 500 Hz is applied. Calculate the output voltage and the phase shift. Also state the −3 dB bandwidth of the filter.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate output voltage:**

$f = 500$ Hz, $f_c = 1000$ Hz, so $f/f_c = 0.5$.

Magnitude of transfer function:

$$\lvert H \rvert = A_v \cdot \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

Since $\omega RC = f / f_c = 0.5$:

$$\lvert H \rvert = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{1 + 0.25}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{1.25}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5}{1.118}$$

$$\lvert H \rvert = 2 \cdot 0.4472 = 0.8944$$

Output voltage:

$$V_{out} = 0.8944 \times 100 \, mV_{p-p} = 89.44 \, mV_{p-p}$$

**Step 2 — Phase shift:**

$$\phi = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5)$$

$$\phi = 90° - 26.57° = +63.43°$$

(Positive phase = output leads input — characteristic of HPF.)

**Step 3 — −3 dB bandwidth:**

For a **first-order HPF**, the −3 dB frequency **is** the cutoff:

$$\boxed{BW = f_c = 1 \, kHz}$$

The filter passes all frequencies **above 1 kHz** within 3 dB of the passband gain of 2 (≈1.414 output for 1 V input). Frequencies below 1 kHz are attenuated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often write $f_c = 1/(RC)$ **without the $2\pi$** — this gives an answer off by a factor of $2\pi \approx 6.28$. Always include $2\pi$.
> 2. The phase of an HPF is **positive** (output leads input), **not negative**. Students confuse this with LPF.
> 3. The −3 dB point refers to a **voltage ratio of $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$**, not exactly 0.5.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]`

#### **(a) Design a Twin-T notch filter to reject 50 Hz mains interference. Use $C = 47 \, nF$. Draw the circuit, derive the transfer function, and explain how the Q-factor is enhanced using an op-amp.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate R for $f_0 = 50$ Hz:**

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.477 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 67.7 \, k\Omega$$

Use standard value $R = 68 \, k\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Symmetric component selection:**

For a balanced Twin-T:
- Low-pass arm: $R_1 = R_2 = 2R = 136 \, k\Omega$, shunt $2C = 94 \, nF$.
- High-pass arm: $C_1 = C_2 = C = 47 \, nF$, shunt $R/2 = 34 \, k\Omega$.

**Step 3 — Transfer function:**

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + 4s\omega_0 + \omega_0^2}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}}$$

**Step 4 — Notch frequency verification:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 68 \times 10^3 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{2.006 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 49.8 \, Hz \approx 50 \, Hz \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Passive Q-factor:**

$$Q_{passive} = 0.25$$

This gives a very wide notch — at 50 Hz ±25 Hz, the rejection is only moderate.

**Step 6 — Q enhancement with op-amp:**

The op-amp is connected as a **non-inverting amplifier** with feedback resistors $R_f$ and $R_i$ such that $A_v < 1$ (e.g., $A_v = 0.95$):

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 0.95 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = -0.05 R_i$$

In practice, a **potentiometer** is used: the wiper taps a fraction of the output back to the input summing node. Setting $A_v = 0.95$:

$$Q_{active} = \frac{1}{4(1 - 0.95)} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$

The notch now has a **bandwidth of $50/5 = 10$ Hz** — a much sharper rejection between 45–55 Hz.

**Step 7 — Circuit description:**

The op-amp's non-inverting input receives the Twin-T output. A fraction of the output is fed back positively to the **center node of the Twin-T**, compensating for losses and boosting Q.

---

#### **(b) For the notch filter designed in (a), calculate the −20 dB bandwidth and the rejection at 49 Hz. Comment on the practical difficulty of tuning.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — −3 dB bandwidth of the notch (Q = 5):**

$$BW_{-3dB} = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \, Hz$$

**Step 2 — −20 dB bandwidth:**

For a 2nd-order notch, the slope of the rejection is approximately **40 dB/decade** on either side of $f_0$ (similar to a 2nd-order system near resonance). To go from 0 dB to −20 dB, we need a frequency deviation corresponding to the transition band.

For a notch filter with Q = 5:

$$\frac{\lvert H \rvert^2}{A_{max}^2} = \frac{1}{1 + Q^2 \left( \dfrac{f}{f_0} - \dfrac{f_0}{f} \right)^2}$$

Setting this to 0.01 (−20 dB):

$$0.01 = \frac{1}{1 + 25 \left( \dfrac{f}{50} - \dfrac{50}{f} \right)^2}$$

$$1 + 25 \left( \dfrac{f}{50} - \dfrac{50}{f} \right)^2 = 100$$

$$\left( \dfrac{f}{50} - \dfrac{50}{f} \right)^2 = \frac{99}{25} = 3.96$$

$$\left( \dfrac{f}{50} - \dfrac{50}{f} \right) = \pm 1.99$$

Solving for $f$ on the lower side (taking positive root, lower solution):

$$\frac{f}{50} - \frac{50}{f} = 1.99$$

Let $x = f/50$:

$$x - \frac{1}{x} = 1.99 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 - 1.99x - 1 = 0$$

$$x = \frac{1.99 \pm \sqrt{1.99^2 + 4}}{2} = \frac{1.99 \pm 2.62}{2}$$

Taking the positive smaller root: $x = (1.99 - 2.62)/2 = -0.315$ (reject — negative).
Taking the positive larger root: $x = (1.99 + 2.62)/2 = 2.305$.

So $f_{low} = 50/2.305 \approx 21.7$ Hz, and on the upper side: $f_{high} = 50 \times 2.305 \approx 115.2$ Hz.

$$\boxed{BW_{-20dB} \approx 115.2 - 21.7 \approx 93.5 \, Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Rejection at 49 Hz:**

$f = 49$ Hz, $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $\Delta f = 1$ Hz.

$$\left( \dfrac{f}{f_0} - \dfrac{f_0}{f} \right) = \frac{49}{50} - \frac{50}{49} = 0.98 - 1.0204 = -0.0404$$

$$\lvert H \rvert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + 25 \times (0.0404)^2} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0408} = \frac{1}{1.0408} = 0.9608$$

$$\lvert H \rvert = 0.980$$

In dB: $20 \log_{10}(0.980) = -0.173 \, dB$

So the rejection at 49 Hz is only **−0.17 dB** — the filter passes 49 Hz almost completely! This demonstrates the **sharp selectivity** of a high-Q notch: only frequencies very close to 50 Hz are rejected.

**Step 4 — Practical tuning difficulty:**

1. **Component tolerance:** Standard capacitors have ±5% or ±10% tolerance. A 47 nF capacitor that is actually 49.4 nF shifts the notch from 50 Hz to 47.6 Hz. The filter must be **trimmed** with a variable capacitor or resistor.
2. **Temperature drift:** TC of capacitors changes $f_0$ with temperature.
3. **Mains frequency variation:** In India, the grid frequency varies from 49.5–50.5 Hz, sometimes drifting the interference **outside** the fixed notch.
4. **Q-enhancement instability:** If $A_v$ is set too close to 1 (e.g., 0.99), the positive feedback can cause the circuit to **oscillate** at $f_0$. A careful balance is required.
5. **Solution:** Modern implementations use **adaptive notch filters** with PLL tracking of the mains frequency.

> [!WARNING]
