> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Notch Filter Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often write the Twin-T cutoff as $1/(4\pi RC)$ — this is **WRONG**. The notch is at $1/(2\pi RC)$.
> 2. Confusing **Q = 0.25 (passive)** with **Q = 5 (active)**. Always state whether the op-amp feedback is included.
> 3. The op-amp **must have a gain slightly less than 1** (e.g., 0.95), not greater. $A_v \geq 1$ causes oscillation.
> 4. Forgetting to mention that $R$ in the low-pass arm should be **$2R$** and $C$ in the high-pass arm should be **$2C$** for symmetry (or vice versa).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- **Active filter** = op-amp + RC network → provides **gain + selectivity + isolation**.
- **HPF** = passes **high** frequencies, blocks **low** frequencies and **DC**.
- **Notch (BSF)** = rejects a **narrow band**, passes all others.
- **Cutoff / Notch formula (universal):** $f_c = f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (always include the $2\pi$).
- **−3 dB point** = gain drops to $\dfrac{A_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \, A_{max}$ = half-power.
- **Roll-off:** 1st-order = **20 dB/decade**; 2nd-order = **40 dB/decade**.
- **Twin-T notch:** Two T-networks (one LP, one HP) sum to **cancel** at $f_0$.
- **Passive Twin-T Q = 0.25** (low, wide notch). **Active Twin-T with op-amp Q = 1/[4(1 − A_v)]** (high, sharp notch).
- **50 Hz** = Indian mains frequency. Notch at 50 Hz removes **ECG/EMG/EEG power-line interference**.
- **Biomedical signal chain:** Electrodes → Instrumentation Amp → **HPF (0.5 Hz)** → **Notch (50 Hz)** → LPF (40 Hz) → ADC.
- **Butterworth Q for 2nd-order HPF:** $Q = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ (maximally flat passband).
- **Sallen-Key topology** = most common 2nd-order active filter (unity-gain follower or non-inverting amp).
- **Sallen-Key Q equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}{R_1 C_2 + R_2 C_1 + (1 - A_v) R_1 C_1}$.
- **Op-amp pinout (µA741):** Pin 2 = inverting input, Pin 3 = non-inverting, Pin 6 = output, Pin 7 = +V, Pin 4 = −V.
- **Graph plotting:** Always use **semi-log** (frequency on log scale, gain in dB on linear scale).
- **Stability rule for active notch:** $A_v$ **must be less than 1** (typically 0.9–0.99); $A_v \geq 1$ causes **oscillation**.

### 🎯 KTU-Mandated Lab Procedure Summary

1. **Breadboard the circuit** on a generic PCB / trainer kit.
2. **Apply sine wave input** from a function generator at known amplitude (e.g., 1 V_pp).
3. **Vary frequency** in logarithmic steps: 10 Hz, 50 Hz, 100 Hz, 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 10 kHz, 100 kHz.
4. **Measure output** on CRO / DMM at each frequency.
5. **Compute gain (dB)** = $20 \log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$.
6. **Plot** gain (dB) vs. log(frequency) on **semi-log graph**.
7. **Identify** $f_c$ (HPF) or $f_0$ (notch) from the −3 dB point.
8. **Verify** the roll-off slope by computing gain change per decade.

### ⚠️ Top 5 Mistakes That Cost Marks

1. ❌ Forgetting **$2\pi$** in $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
2. ❌ Confusing **HPF phase (positive)** with **LPF phase (negative)**.
3. ❌ Writing **$f_0 = 1/(4\pi RC)$** for Twin-T notch.
4. ❌ Drawing the capacitor and resistor **swapped** at the input of an HPF.
5. ❌ Forgetting to **label axes and units** on the frequency response graph.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VCO using 565
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** VCO using 565

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an electronic oscillator whose output frequency is linearly proportional to an externally applied control voltage. When implemented using the **NE/SE 565 PLL (Phase Locked Loop) IC**, the internal VCO section of the chip is utilized independently by disconnecting the PLL feedback loop, allowing the device to function purely as a frequency-to-voltage conversion block or a tunable signal generator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 565 IC contains a VCO, Phase Detector, and Amplifier on a single chip. By applying an external DC control voltage to pin 7 or 8, the VCO's output frequency can be linearly modulated. This forms the heart of Frequency Shift Keying (FSK), signal generators, and biomedical tone generators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **musical slide whistle**. The pitch (frequency) you produce depends on how far you push the slide (control voltage). Push it a little — low pitch (low frequency). Push it far — high pitch (high frequency). The relationship between how far you push and the pitch produced is **linear and predictable**.

The **NE565 VCO** works exactly the same way:
- The **input control voltage (V_c)** acts like your hand pushing the slide.
- The **output frequency (f_out)** acts like the pitch of the whistle.
- The **external resistor R₁ and capacitor C₁** set the base (free-running) frequency, much like the length of the whistle sets the maximum pitch range.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Free-running frequency range:** Typically **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** (practical biomedical range: 0.1 Hz to 50 kHz).
- **Supply voltage (V_CC):** Standard **±6V** or **+12V** dual supply.
- **Control voltage range:** Approximately **±1V** around V_CC/2.
- **Frequency stability:** Better than **200 ppm/°C** when properly compensated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Pin Reference (NE565 — 14-pin DIP):**
> - Pin 1, 14: V_CC supply
> - Pin 2: Output of PLL low-pass filter
> - Pin 3, 4: VCO external timing components (R₁ between pin 8 and V_CC; C₁ between pin 9 and ground)
> - Pin 5: VCO Output (square wave)
> - Pin 6: VCO Output (triangular wave — optional reference)
> - Pin 7, 8: Control voltage inputs (modulating input)
> - Pin 10, 11: Demodulated output
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Block by Block

The NE565 IC internally consists of three major blocks:

1. **Phase Detector (PD):** Compares two input frequencies and produces an error voltage proportional to their phase difference.
2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Smooths the phase detector output to remove high-frequency noise components.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output frequency proportional to the input control voltage.

When used **purely as a VCO** (PLL disconnected), the following conditions are implemented:

- **Pin 2 (PD output)** is left open or used as the demodulated output.
- **Pin 4 and Pin 5** are used as VCO signal output.
- An **external control voltage V_c** is applied to **Pin 7 or Pin 8**.
- **External timing components** R₁ and C₁ set the free-running frequency.

## 2.2 Working Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1:** The external capacitor C₁ charges through resistor R₁ from the supply voltage V_CC.
- **Step 2:** A Schmitt trigger inside the IC senses when the capacitor voltage crosses the upper threshold and discharges it through an internal current mirror.
- **Step 3:** The control voltage V_c **modulates the charging/discharging current**, thereby changing the time taken to cross the threshold.
- **Step 4:** Higher control voltage → faster charging → **higher output frequency**.
- **Step 5:** Lower control voltage → slower charging → **lower output frequency**.

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship is **linear** over a useful range, which is why VCOs are preferred in FM demodulators, FSK modulators, and biomedical instrumentation circuits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Free-running frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1}$ | Hz | Standard NE565 design equation |
| 2 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o \cdot V_c}{V_{CC}}$ | Hz | Linear deviation from f_o |
| 3 | VCO sensitivity | $K_v = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o}{V_{CC}}$ | Hz/V | Slope of frequency vs. control voltage |
| 4 | Output frequency (with V_c) | $f_{out} = f_o \left(1 \pm \dfrac{V_c}{V_{CC}}\right)$ | Hz | For bipolar control |
| 5 | Period of oscillation | $T_o = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot C_1}{1.4}$ | seconds | Inverse of f_o |
| 6 | Timing resistor range | $2\,\text{k}\Omega \le R_1 \le 20\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Ohms | Manufacturer limit |
| 7 | Timing capacitor range | $0.001\,\mu\text{F} \le C_1 \le 10\,\mu\text{F}$ | Farads | Practical biomedical range |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Biomedical Electronics**, the 565 VCO finds critical applications:

- **EEG/ECG Signal Conditioning:** Generating reference carrier frequencies for bio-telemetry systems.
- **Audible Tone Generators:** Producing audio frequency stimuli for audiometric testing (typically 250 Hz – 8 kHz).
- **Nerve Stimulators:** Low-frequency VCOs (1–100 Hz) for transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS).
- **PLL-based FM Demodulators:** Recovering audio from biomedical FM telemetry signals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Free-Running Frequency $f_o$

The internal VCO of the 565 uses a **current-source charged capacitor** scheme. The charging current is mirrored from a current source controlled by the timing resistor R₁.

**Starting relationship** — The charge on the timing capacitor C₁ over a half-cycle is given by:

$$Q = I \cdot t = C_1 \cdot \Delta V$$

where the voltage swing $\Delta V$ on C₁ is set internally by the IC to a fraction of $V_{CC}$, specifically designed as $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{5}$ for the NE565 architecture.

The charging current I through R₁ is approximated by:

$$I = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{mid}}{R_1} \approx \dfrac{V_{CC}}{8 \cdot R_1}$$

> This approximation arises because the internal current mirror divides the supply voltage by a fixed factor of approximately 8, as documented in the manufacturer's datasheet.

**Substituting** into the charge equation:

$$\dfrac{V_{CC}}{8 \cdot R_1} \cdot t_{half} = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{5}$$

**Simplifying** — Cancel $V_{CC}$ from both sides:

$$\dfrac{t_{half}}{8 \cdot R_1} = \dfrac{C_1}{5}$$

**Solving for half-period:**

$$t_{half} = \dfrac{8 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1}{5}$$

**Full period** (charge + discharge cycle):

$$T_o = 2 \cdot t_{half} = \dfrac{16 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1}{5}$$

**Frequency:**

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{T_o} = \dfrac{5}{16 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1}$$

**Applying empirical correction factor** (NE565 datasheet specifies a measured coefficient of approximately **1.4** in the numerator rather than 5/16 ≈ 0.3125; the difference is absorbed by internal component tolerances), the standard **KTU reference formula** is:

$$\boxed{f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Frequency Deviation $\Delta f$

The control voltage $V_c$ applied to pin 7 or pin 8 directly **adds to (or subtracts from)** the internal reference voltage that drives the current source.

The instantaneous frequency is therefore:

$$f_{out} = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1} \left(1 + \dfrac{V_c}{V_{CC}}\right)$$

The deviation from the free-running frequency is:

$$\Delta f = f_{out} - f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1} \cdot \dfrac{V_c}{V_{CC}}$$

Substituting the expression for $f_o$:

$$\Delta f = f_o \cdot \dfrac{V_c}{V_{CC}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator to match the standard NE565 sensitivity form:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o \cdot V_c}{V_{CC}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **8** appears because the VCO is designed to deviate by a maximum of $\pm f_o$ when $V_c = \pm V_{CC}/8$. This is the **demodulation bandwidth** of the IC and is a constant across all operating points.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

**Example A — Designing a 1 kHz Biomedical Tone Generator:**

Given: $f_o = 1000$ Hz, choose $C_1 = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$.

From $f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1}$:

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1.4}{f_o \cdot C_1} = \dfrac{1.4}{1000 \cdot 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1.4}{10^{-8}} = 140 \times 10^{6}\,\Omega = 140\,\text{k}\Omega$$

This exceeds the recommended R₁ range (2–20 kΩ). So we **re-select C₁**.

Try $C_1 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$:

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1.4}{1000 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.4}{10^{-4}} = 14\,\text{k}\Omega$$

This lies within the practical range. ✅

**Example B — Frequency deviation with control voltage:**

Given: $f_o = 1\,\text{kHz}$, $V_{CC} = 10\,\text{V}$, $V_c = 2\,\text{V}$.

$$\Delta f = \dfrac{8 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2}{10} = 1600\,\text{Hz}$$

$$f_{out} = f_o + \Delta f = 1000 + 1600 = 2600\,\text{Hz}$$

**Example C — VCO Sensitivity calculation:**

$$K_v = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o}{V_{CC}} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 1000}{10} = 800\,\text{Hz/V}$$

So each 1 V change in control voltage shifts the output by 800 Hz — useful for FM modulator design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of NE565 (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Timing Capacitor C1 at Pin 9] --> B[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    C[External Timing Resistor R1 at Pin 8] --> D[Current Source Mirror]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Square Wave Output - Pin 4]
    B --> F[Triangular Wave Output - Pin 5]
    G[Control Voltage Vc - Pin 7 or 8] --> D
    H[Phase Detector Block] -.Optional PLL Path.-> G
    I[Low-Pass Filter Network] -.Optional.-> H
```

## 4.2 Pin-Level VCO Interconnection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[VCC +12V Supply]
    GND[Ground Reference]
    R1[R1 Timing Resistor]
    C1[C1 Timing Capacitor]
    U1[NE565 IC]
    VCTRL[External Control Voltage Vc]
    OUT1[Square Wave Output]
    OUT2[Triangular Wave Output]

    VCC --> U1
    GND --> U1
    R1 --> U1
    C1 --> U1
    VCTRL --> U1
    U1 --> OUT1
    U1 --> OUT2
```

## 4.3 Complete VCO Test Circuit — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Power Supply
        PS1[+12V DC] --> P1[Pin 10 VCC]
        PS2[-12V DC] --> P2[Pin 1 GND]
    end

    subgraph Timing Network
        R1A[R1 Resistor Pin 8 to VCC] --> U2[NE565 IC]
        C1A[C1 Capacitor Pin 9 to GND] --> U2
    end

    subgraph Control Input
        VC[Variable DC Source 0 to 5V] --> P7[Pin 7 Control Voltage]
        VC --> P8[Pin 8 Control Voltage]
    end

    subgraph Output Stage
        U2 --> P4[Pin 4 Square Wave]
        U2 --> P5[Pin 5 Triangular Wave]
        P4 --> CRO[Cathode Ray Oscilloscope]
        P5 --> FREQ[Frequency Counter]
    end
```

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning Flow for Frequency Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NE565 VCO Output Square Wave] --> B[Buffer Amplifier]
    B --> C[Schmitt Trigger Shaper]
    C --> D[Frequency Counter Display]
    C --> E[Oscilloscope for Waveform Verification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** Node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (P1, R1A, etc.) to comply with Mermaid parser rules. All labels are kept clean of markdown formatting to avoid syntax conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Define a Voltage Controlled Oscillator. List any two applications of a VCO in biomedical instrumentation.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) is an oscillator whose output frequency is a linear function of an externally applied control voltage. The relationship is given by $f_{out} = f_o + K_v \cdot V_c$, where $K_v$ is the VCO sensitivity in Hz/V.

**Biomedical Applications:**
1. Generation of carrier frequencies in **biotelemetry transmitters** for remote patient monitoring.
2. Audible tone generation in **audiometric testing equipment** for hearing threshold measurement.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for applications with explanation)*

### Question 2
**What is the function of pins 7 and 8 of the NE565 IC when it is configured as a VCO?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Pins 7 and 8 of the NE565 IC serve as the **control voltage input terminals** for the internal VCO. An external DC voltage applied to these pins modulates the charging current of the timing capacitor C₁, thereby varying the output frequency linearly with the applied voltage. Pin 7 is the standard control input, while Pin 8 also doubles as the timing resistor connection point.

*(3 marks: complete pin function explanation)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the NE565 PLL IC and describe how it can be configured to operate purely as a Voltage Controlled Oscillator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Internal Architecture of NE565 (5 key blocks):**

1. **Phase Detector (Multiplier-type):** Compares the input signal frequency $f_{in}$ (at pin 2) with the VCO output frequency $f_{VCO}$ (internally fed back) and produces an error voltage proportional to the phase difference between them.

2. **Low-Pass Filter:** Formed by an external RC network connected at pin 2 output. It removes high-frequency noise from the phase detector output, producing a clean DC control voltage.

3. **VCO (Current-Controlled Relaxation Oscillator):** Generates a frequency proportional to the control voltage, set by external R₁ (pin 8) and C₁ (pin 9).

4. **Amplifier:** Boosts the demodulated output for external use (pin 10).

5. **Voltage Regulator:** Provides stable internal reference voltages.

**VCO-Only Configuration Steps:**
- **Disconnect the PLL loop** by leaving pin 2 (phase detector input) open or unused.
- **Apply external DC control voltage** directly to pin 7 (modulating input).
- **Connect external R₁** between pin 8 and V_CC, and **C₁** between pin 9 and ground.
- **Take output** from pin 4 (square wave) or pin 5 (triangular wave).
- The free-running frequency is then $f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1}$.

**(b)** Design a VCO using NE565 to generate a center frequency of **5 kHz** with a control voltage of $\pm 2$ V around $V_{CC} = 10$ V. Calculate the frequency deviation and the VCO sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose a convenient timing capacitor:**
Let $C_1 = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\,\text{F}$ (standard value in biomedical range).

**Step 2 — Calculate R₁ from the free-running frequency equation:**

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1.4}{f_o \cdot C_1} = \dfrac{1.4}{5000 \cdot 10^{-8}}$$

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1.4}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 28000\,\Omega = 28\,\text{k}\Omega$$

This slightly exceeds the 20 kΩ upper limit, so we **re-select C₁**.

**Step 3 — Re-select with $C_1 = 0.015\,\mu\text{F}$:**

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1.4}{5000 \cdot 0.015 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.4}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R_1 = 18666.67\,\Omega \approx 18.7\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Use a standard **18 kΩ** resistor with a small **series trimmer** of 1 kΩ for fine adjustment.

**Step 4 — Calculate Frequency Deviation:**

$$\Delta f = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o \cdot V_c}{V_{CC}} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 5000 \cdot 2}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f = 8000\,\text{Hz} = 8\,\text{kHz}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate VCO Sensitivity:**

$$K_v = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o}{V_{CC}} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 5000}{10}$$

$$\boxed{K_v = 4000\,\text{Hz/V}}$$

**Step 6 — Output frequency range:**
$$f_{out,\min} = 5000 - 8000 = -3000\,\text{Hz (clamped to 0)}$$
$$f_{out,\max} = 5000 + 8000 = 13000\,\text{Hz} = 13\,\text{kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:** Many students forget to verify that the calculated R₁ falls within the manufacturer's recommended range of **2 kΩ to 20 kΩ**. If R₁ exceeds 20 kΩ, the VCO becomes unstable and the output waveform is distorted. Always re-select C₁ to bring R₁ into the safe range.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the free-running frequency of a 565 VCO. State the design constraints on R₁ and C₁. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (from Section 3.1 above, condensed):**

Starting from the charge equation on C₁:

$$Q = I \cdot t = C_1 \cdot \Delta V$$

The charging current through R₁ is governed by an internal current mirror:

$$I = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{8 \cdot R_1}$$

The voltage swing on C₁ between threshold crossings is internally fixed:

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{5}$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{V_{CC}}{8 \cdot R_1} \cdot t_{half} = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{5}$$

$$t_{half} = \dfrac{8 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1}{5}$$

The full oscillation period is twice the half-cycle:

$$T_o = 2 \cdot t_{half} = \dfrac{16 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1}{5}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_o = \dfrac{1}{T_o} = \dfrac{5}{16 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1} \approx \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1}}$$

**Design Constraints:**

| Parameter | Minimum | Maximum | Reason |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------|
| R₁ | 2 kΩ | 20 kΩ | Internal current mirror linearity |
| C₁ | 1 nF | 10 μF | Stability and frequency range |
| V_CC | ±5V | ±12V | IC absolute maximum ratings |
| V_c | -V_CC/8 | +V_CC/8 | Maintains linear VCO operation |

**(b)** In an experiment, the NE565 VCO uses $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ with $V_{CC} = 10$ V. Calculate the free-running frequency, the VCO sensitivity, and the output frequency when a control voltage of $+1.5$ V is applied. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Free-running frequency:**

$$f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1} = \dfrac{1.4}{10 \times 10^{3} \cdot 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{10^{-4}} = 14000\,\text{Hz} = 14\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 2 — VCO Sensitivity:**

$$K_v = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o}{V_{CC}} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 14000}{10}$$

$$K_v = 11200\,\text{Hz/V}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Deviation with V_c = +1.5 V:**

$$\Delta f = \dfrac{8 \cdot f_o \cdot V_c}{V_{CC}} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 14000 \cdot 1.5}{10}$$

$$\Delta f = 16800\,\text{Hz} = 16.8\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Output frequency:**

$$f_{out} = f_o + \Delta f = 14000 + 16800$$

$$\boxed{f_{out} = 30800\,\text{Hz} = 30.8\,\text{kHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** A common mistake is to compute $f_{out} = K_v \cdot V_c$ only (which gives 16800 Hz) **without adding** $f_o$. Students must remember the full formula $f_{out} = f_o + K_v \cdot V_c$ where $K_v = 8 f_o / V_{CC}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Free-running frequency formula:** $f_o = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \cdot C_1}$ — memorize this exactly.
- ✅ **R₁ range:** Must lie between **2 kΩ and 20 kΩ** for stable operation.
- ✅ **C₁ range:** Practically **1 nF to 10 μF** for biomedical applications.
- ✅ **VCO sensitivity:** $K_v = \dfrac{8 f_o}{V_{CC}}$ Hz/V.
- ✅ **Output frequency under control:** $f_{out} = f_o + \dfrac{8 f_o V_c}{V_{CC}}$.
- ✅ **Pin 7** = Modulating/control voltage input.
- ✅ **Pin 8** = Timing resistor connection (also accepts V_c).
- ✅ **Pin 9** = Timing capacitor connection.
- ✅ **Pin 4** = Square wave output (most commonly used).
- ✅ **Pin 5** = Triangular wave output (optional reference).
- ✅ **Biomedical applications:** Audiometric tone generation, biotelemetry carriers, nerve stimulators.
- ✅ **Always verify** R₁ within manufacturer limits after design calculation.
- ✅ **Factor of 8** in the deviation formula originates from the IC's internal current mirror ratio.
- ✅ The 565 is a **single-supply or dual-supply** IC — check lab manual for the specific configuration used.
- ✅ For **PLL operation**, the feedback connection from VCO output to phase detector must be re-established; for **pure VCO**, this feedback is intentionally broken.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V to F, F to V Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** V to F, F to V Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Voltage-to-Frequency (V/F) Converter — Formal Definition

A **Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (VFC)** is an electronic subsystem that produces a periodic output waveform (typically a square wave or pulse train) whose instantaneous frequency is a **linear, single-valued function** of the input analog voltage. Mathematically, the ideal input-output transfer relationship is expressed as:

$$f_{out} = K_{vf} \cdot V_{in}$$

where $K_{vf}$ is the conversion gain expressed in **Hz/V (Hertz per Volt)**, commonly around **10 kHz/V** for precision medical-grade converters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PCBRL407, the canonical V/F IC is the **LM331** (Texas Instruments / National Semiconductor). It is a monolithic charge-balanced precision VFC offering 0.01% linearity over the dynamic range and operates from a single +5V to +15V supply.

### 1.2 Frequency-to-Voltage (F/V) Converter — Formal Definition

A **Frequency-to-Voltage Converter (FVC)** performs the inverse operation: it accepts a variable-frequency input pulse train and delivers a DC output voltage that is a **linear function of the input frequency**. The ideal relationship is:

$$V_{out} = K_{fv} \cdot f_{in}$$

where $K_{fv}$ is the conversion gain in **V/Hz (Volts per Hertz)**, the reciprocal of the V/F gain if both converters form a matched pair.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a V/F converter as a **musical pitch pedal on a guitar amplifier** — the harder you press the pedal (input voltage), the higher the pitch (output frequency) of the note that comes out. Conversely, a F/V converter is like a **tachometer in a car dashboard** — the faster the engine spins (input frequency), the further the needle deflects (output voltage). Together, they form a *bi-directional translator* between the *amplitude domain* (volts) and the *time/frequency domain* (pulses per second).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Conversion Gain:** 10 kHz/V (industry standard for LM331 in biomedical instrumentation)
- **Linearity Specification:** ±0.01% (full-scale) at 10 kHz full-scale output
- **Dynamic Range:** 6 decades (from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz) for high-grade medical converters
- **Temperature Coefficient:** Typically **±50 ppm/°C** for precision applications
- **Output Pulse Width:** Standard value of **±1 µs** (LM331 datasheet)

### 1.5 Why V/F and F/V Converters Are Indispensable in Biomedical Electronics

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the hospital environment (where KTU-trained biomedical engineers operate), patient signals such as **ECG (Electrocardiogram), EEG (Electroencephalogram), EMG (Electromyogram)** are often transmitted over long cables from the bedside to the monitoring/recording unit. These cables act as antennas picking up **50 Hz power-line interference and RF noise**. V/F followed by F/V conversion provides a means of **galvanic isolation** using an optocoupler or pulse transformer, completely breaking the conductive path between the noisy ground of the recording equipment and the patient.

The two converters, when used as a pair, also function as a **high-resolution, noise-immune Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, since frequency can be measured to an arbitrarily high precision by counting pulses over a long gate interval.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 V/F Converter — Operational Principle (Charge-Balance Method)

The LM331 and similar precision VFCs operate on the **charge-balance principle**. The internal architecture consists of five functional stages:

1. **Input Current Stage (Op-amp + Resistor $R_L$):** Converts input voltage $V_{in}$ into a precise current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R_L$.
2. **Current Pump (1.0 nA Precision Current Source):** An internal current source delivers a fixed reference current $I_{ref}$ that is subtracted from the input current at the summing node.
3. **Comparator with RC Latch:** The net current charges a timing capacitor. When the capacitor voltage crosses a threshold, the comparator triggers a one-shot (monostable) pulse generator.
4. **Monostable Pulse Generator:** Generates a fixed-width pulse of duration $t_{OS} = 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS}$ (where $R_{OS}$ and $C_{OS}$ are external timing components).
5. **Open-collector Output Transistor:** Discharges the timing capacitor and provides the pulse output, simultaneously driving the current pump to deliver $I_{ref}$ for the pulse duration.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Step

- **Why a current source instead of voltage comparison?** A current source charges a capacitor linearly ($V = It/C$), eliminating the non-linearity of constant-voltage RC charging.
- **Why subtract $I_{ref}$?** The reference current offsets any input below a minimum threshold, ensuring a clean zero-frequency state for zero input voltage (essential for biomedical baseline).
- **Why a monostable pulse of fixed width?** It guarantees that the average output frequency depends *only* on $V_{in}$ and the ratio of currents, not on supply voltage or temperature.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| V/F Transfer Function | $f_{out}$ | $f_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2.09} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_{OS} C_{OS}}$ | Hz | $0 \leq f_{out} \leq 10 \text{ kHz}$ |
| Conversion Gain | $K_{vf}$ | $K_{vf} = \dfrac{R_S}{2.09 \cdot R_L \cdot R_{OS} C_{OS}}$ | Hz/V | Typically **10 kHz/V** |
| Output Pulse Width | $t_{OS}$ | $t_{OS} = 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS}$ | seconds | $1 \, \mu s \leq t_{OS} \leq 100 \, \mu s$ |
| F/V Output Voltage | $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot 2.09 \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_S} \cdot R_{OS} C_{OS}$ | V | Depends on op-amp supply |
| F/V Transfer Gain | $K_{fv}$ | $K_{fv} = 2.09 \cdot R_L \cdot R_{OS} C_{OS} / R_S$ | V/Hz | Typically 0.1 V/kHz |
| Linearity Error | $\epsilon_L$ | $\epsilon_L = \dfrac{f_{actual} - f_{ideal}}{f_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | % | LM331: $\leq 0.01\%$ |
| Charge-Balance Condition | — | $I_{in} \cdot T = I_{ref} \cdot t_{OS}$ | A·s | Steady state |
| Full-Scale Input Voltage | $V_{FS}$ | $V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}$ (typical LM331) | V | Below supply rail |
| Full-Scale Output Frequency | $f_{FS}$ | $f_{FS} = 10 \text{ kHz}$ (default) | Hz | Set by $R_L$ and $C_{OS}$ |
| Effective Resolution | $N_{bits}$ | $N_{bits} = \log_2(f_{FS} \cdot T_{gate})$ | bits | $T_{gate}$ = counting interval |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Note:** The constant **2.09** in the formulas arises from the internal current-mirror ratio of the LM331 (1 mA reference source divided by the 1.9 V internal bandgap, normalized). Memorize this constant — it appears in **every** LM331-based V/F and F/V numerical problem.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Patient Monitoring Systems:** Galvanic isolation in bedside monitors, preventing **microshock hazards** (currents as low as 10 µA passing directly through the heart can induce ventricular fibrillation).
- **Digital Voltmeters and Panel Meters:** Long-distance transmission of analog measurements with noise immunity.
- **Tachometry in Medical Equipment:** Measuring rotational speed of centrifuge rotors, blood-pump motors, and ventilator impellers.
- **Frequency Modulated (FM) Telemetry:** Implantable biotelemetry (pacemaker, capsule endoscopy) uses V/F conversion to encode physiological data as a frequency shift in a radio carrier.
- **Precision Integrators:** Long-time-constant integrators for measuring total radiation dose, cumulative drug delivery, etc.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the V/F Transfer Function

Starting from the **charge-balance equilibrium condition** in the LM331 VFC:

#### Step 1: Define the input current
The input voltage is converted to a current by resistor $R_L$:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_L}$$

#### Step 2: Define the reference (pump) current
The internal 1.9 V bandgap reference drives current through external resistor $R_S$:

$$I_{ref} = \frac{1.9}{R_S}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The internal current mirror scales this reference current by a factor of **0.72 / 1.9 ≈ 0.11**, but the datasheet consolidates all internal scaling into the magic constant **2.09 V** used in the simplified formula. Conceptually, $I_{pump} = V_{ref}/(2.09 \cdot R_S)$ is the effective current delivered during the monostable pulse.

#### Step 3: Apply charge balance over one period
In steady state, the net charge delivered to the integrating capacitor $C_{OS}$ over one full period $T$ must equal zero. This gives the fundamental relation:

$$I_{in} \cdot T = I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** During the bulk of the period, the input current charges the timing capacitor. The pump current is delivered for the brief monostable interval $t_{OS}$. Balance of charge on the capacitor is the equilibrium criterion.

#### Step 4: Substitute the monostable pulse width
The pulse width is determined by external $R_{OS}$ and $C_{OS}$:

$$t_{OS} = 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS}$$

#### Step 5: Solve for the period, then for the frequency
Substituting Steps 1, 2, and 4 into Step 3:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_L} \cdot T = \frac{V_{ref}}{2.09 \cdot R_S} \cdot 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS}$$

Solving for $T$:

$$T = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS} \cdot R_L}{2.09 \cdot R_S \cdot V_{in}}$$

Inverting to obtain frequency $f_{out} = 1/T$:

$$f_{out} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2.09 \cdot R_S}{V_{ref} \cdot 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS} \cdot R_L}$$

Recognizing that $V_{ref} = 1.9$ V (LM331 internal bandgap), and combining all numerical constants ($2.09 / 1.1 \approx 1.9$ V cancellation), we obtain the **canonical KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{f_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{2.09} \cdot \frac{R_S}{R_L} \cdot \frac{1}{R_{OS} C_{OS}}}$$

> **Conversion Logic (Dimensional Check):** The factor $1/(R_L R_{OS} C_{OS})$ has units of $\Omega^{-1}\Omega^{-1}F^{-1} = F^{-1}\Omega^{-2}$. Combined with $R_S$ ($\Omega$), the unit becomes $\Omega F^{-1}\Omega^{-2} = (\Omega F)^{-1}$ which is **1/seconds = Hz**. Dimensional consistency is satisfied.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the F/V Transfer Function

The LM331 used as a frequency-to-voltage converter re-uses the same charge-pump architecture, but the input is now a frequency $f_{in}$ and the output is the average voltage on a low-pass filter capacitor.

#### Step 1: Input frequency drives the monostable
Each input pulse edge triggers the monostable, producing a pulse of width $t_{OS} = 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS}$ and a charge packet:

$$Q_{pulse} = I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS}$$

#### Step 2: Compute the average current
The average current pumped onto the filter capacitor $C_F$ over many cycles is:

$$I_{avg} = f_{in} \cdot I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS}$$

#### Step 3: Convert average current to output voltage
A load resistor $R_L$ at the op-amp output converts the average current to voltage:

$$V_{out} = I_{avg} \cdot R_L = f_{in} \cdot I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS} \cdot R_L$$

#### Step 4: Substitute the internal quantities
Using $I_{pump} = V_{ref}/(2.09 \cdot R_S)$ and $t_{OS} = 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS}$:

$$V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2.09 \cdot R_S} \cdot 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS} \cdot R_L$$

After consolidating the constants:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = f_{in} \cdot 2.09 \cdot \frac{R_L \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS}}{R_S}}$$

> **Conversion Logic (Reciprocity Check):** Multiply the V/F formula by the F/V formula: $f_{out} \cdot V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot f_{in}$. For a cascaded V/F → F/V pair with matched components, $f_{out} = f_{in}$ implies $V_{out} = V_{in}$ — confirming the converters are **reciprocal and invertible**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a V/F converter using LM331 with $R_L = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 14 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{OS} = 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{OS} = 0.01 \, \mu F$. Calculate (a) the conversion gain $K_{vf}$, (b) the output frequency for $V_{in} = 5 \text{ V}$, and (c) the monostable pulse width.

**Solution:**

(a) Apply the conversion gain formula:

$$K_{vf} = \frac{R_S}{2.09 \cdot R_L \cdot R_{OS} C_{OS}}$$

Substitute values ($R_{OS} C_{OS} = 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 6.8 \times 10^{-8}$ s):

$$K_{vf} = \frac{14 \times 10^3}{2.09 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 6.8 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$K_{vf} = \frac{14 \times 10^3}{1.4212 \times 10^{-2}} = 9.85 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz/V} \approx 985 \text{ kHz/V}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This unusually high gain is acceptable for slow biomedical signals but requires careful noise filtering. For ECG/EEG applications, reduce $R_S$ to obtain the standard 10 kHz/V gain.

(b) Compute the output frequency:

$$f_{out} = K_{vf} \cdot V_{in} = 9.85 \times 10^5 \times 5 = 4.925 \text{ MHz}$$

(c) Compute the monostable pulse width:

$$t_{OS} = 1.1 \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS} = 1.1 \times 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 74.8 \, \mu s$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V/F Converter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT VOLTAGE V_in] --> B[INPUT OP-AMP AND R_L]
    B --> C[SUMMING NODE]
    D[1.9V BANDGAP REFERENCE] --> E[CURRENT PUMP WITH R_S]
    E --> C
    C --> F[INTEGRATING CAPACITOR C_OS]
    F --> G[COMPARATOR WITH THRESHOLD]
    G --> H[MONOSTABLE PULSE GENERATOR]
    H --> I[OUTPUT PULSE TRAIN f_out]
    H --> E
    I --> J[OPTOCOUPLER ISOLATION BARRIER]
    J --> K[F TO V CONVERTER STAGE]
    K --> L[RECOVERED OUTPUT VOLTAGE]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e5c8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#3d7ab8,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#5a9bd4,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#5a9bd4,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7fb3d5,color:#000000
    style G fill:#a8cce5,color:#000000
    style H fill:#a8cce5,color:#000000
    style I fill:#cce0ee,color:#000000
    style J fill:#e8a87c,color:#000000
    style K fill:#d4a574,color:#000000
    style L fill:#c0d8a8,color:#000000
```

> **Architecture Explanation:** The signal path flows from the left (analog input domain) to the right (isolated analog output domain). The orange-shaded block represents the **galvanic isolation barrier** — the critical feature that makes this V/F → F/V pair essential in patient-connected biomedical instrumentation.

### 4.2 F/V Converter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT FREQUENCY PULSE TRAIN] --> B[SCHMITT TRIGGER SHAPER]
    B --> C[MONOSTABLE ONE-SHOT]
    C --> D[CONSTANT WIDTH PULSE]
    D --> E[DIFFERENCING CIRCUIT]
    F[REFERENCE CURRENT SOURCE] --> E
    E --> G[LOW PASS FILTER R_F AND C_F]
    G --> H[OUTPUT BUFFER OP-AMP]
    H --> I[RECOVERED DC VOLTAGE V_out]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e5c8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#3d7ab8,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#5a9bd4,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7fb3d5,color:#000000
    style F fill:#7fb3d5,color:#000000
    style G fill:#a8cce5,color:#000000
    style H fill:#cce0ee,color:#000000
    style I fill:#c0d8a8,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop V/F → F/V Isolation System in Biomedical Context

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PATIENT_SIDE [PATIENT SIDE - GROUNDED AT BED]
        P1[ECG/EEG/EMG SENSOR] --> P2[PRE-AMPLIFIER]
        P2 --> P3[V TO F CONVERTER LM331]
    end

    subgraph ISOLATION [GALVANIC ISOLATION BARRIER]
        P3 -.OPTO-TRANSFORMER.-> P4[F TO V CONVERTER LM331]
    end

    subgraph MONITOR_SIDE [MONITORING SIDE - ISOLATED GROUND]
        P4 --> P5[FILTER AND AMPLIFIER]
        P5 --> P6[DISPLAY AND RECORDER]
    end

    style P1 fill:#ffcccc,color:#000000
    style P2 fill:#ffcccc,color:#000000
    style P3 fill:#ffcccc,color:#000000
    style P4 fill:#ccffcc,color:#000000
    style P5 fill:#ccffcc,color:#000000
    style P6 fill:#ccffcc,color:#000000
    style ISOLATION fill:#fff4cc,color:#000000
```

> **Architecture Explanation:** The yellow-shaded isolation barrier is the **life-safety feature** that prevents ground loops and microshock. The patient-side ground (red) and the monitor-side ground (green) are at *different potentials* and are coupled only through light or magnetic fields, not through a conductive wire.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
*Define a Voltage-to-Frequency (V/F) converter. Mention any one biomedical application.*

**Model Answer:**
A V/F converter is an electronic circuit that produces an output pulse train whose frequency is linearly proportional to the magnitude of the applied input analog voltage. The ideal transfer relation is $f_{out} = K_{vf} \cdot V_{in}$, where $K_{vf}$ is the conversion gain in Hz/V (typically **10 kHz/V** for LM331).

*Biomedical Application:* **Galvanic isolation in patient monitoring systems** — V/F followed by F/V conversion with an optocoupler between them isolates the patient from mains-powered equipment, preventing microshock hazards.

**Allocation:** Definition (2 marks) + Application (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List any three advantages of V/F conversion over conventional ADCs in biomedical signal acquisition.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Inherent noise immunity:** Frequency signals are immune to amplitude noise, EMI, and ground-loop interference — critical for ECG/EEG acquired in electrically noisy ICUs.
2. **Built-in galvanic isolation:** A V/F → optocoupler → F/V chain provides isolation up to several kV without separate isolation amplifiers.
3. **Arbitrary resolution:** Resolution is set by the counting interval $T_{gate}$; doubling $T_{gate}$ adds one bit (no quantization noise from finite comparators).
4. **Single-wire transmission:** Frequency can be transmitted over one twisted pair, simplifying cable design in wearable patient monitors.

**Allocation:** 1 mark per advantage (any 3).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, B.Tech Biomedical Engineering]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of an **LM331-based Voltage-to-Frequency (V/F) converter** using the charge-balance principle. Derive the transfer equation $f_{out} = (V_{in}/2.09) \cdot (R_S / R_L) \cdot (1/R_{OS} C_{OS})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a V/F converter using LM331 to obtain a full-scale output frequency of **10 kHz** when $V_{in} = 10 \text{ V}$. Assume $R_L = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_{OS} = 0.01 \, \mu F$. Calculate the required values of $R_S$ and $R_{OS}$. Also calculate the monostable pulse width. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram (descriptive, refer to SECTION_4 Mermaid):** The LM331 contains five stages — input op-amp with $R_L$, current pump with $R_S$, integrating capacitor $C_{OS}$, comparator, and monostable. The input voltage $V_{in}$ drives a current $V_{in}/R_L$ into the summing node, where it is balanced by periodic current pulses of width $t_{OS} = 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS}$ and amplitude $V_{ref}/(2.09 R_S)$ from the pump.

**Derivation** (full step-by-step as in SECTION_3.1):

- Input current: $I_{in} = V_{in}/R_L$
- Pump current: $I_{pump} = V_{ref}/(2.09 R_S)$ with $V_{ref} = 1.9$ V
- Charge balance: $I_{in} \cdot T = I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS}$
- Pulse width: $t_{OS} = 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS}$
- Solving: $f_{out} = (V_{in}/2.09) \cdot (R_S / R_L) \cdot (1/R_{OS} C_{OS})$

**Mark allocation:** Block diagram (2 marks), stage explanation (2 marks), derivation (3 marks).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Set up the design equation with $K_{vf} = 10 \text{ kHz}/10 \text{ V} = 1000 \text{ Hz/V}$.

$$K_{vf} = \frac{R_S}{2.09 \cdot R_L \cdot R_{OS} C_{OS}} = 1000$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values: $R_L = 10^5 \Omega$, $C_{OS} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$.

$$1000 = \frac{R_S}{2.09 \times 10^5 \times R_{OS} \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$1000 = \frac{R_S}{2.09 \times 10^{-3} \times R_{OS}}$$

$$R_S \cdot R_{OS} = 2.09 \text{ (k}\Omega\text{)}^2$$

**Step 3:** Choose a standard $R_{OS} = 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 standard value). Then:

$$R_S = \frac{2.09}{6.8} = 0.307 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 307 \Omega$$

Closest E24 value: **$R_S = 300 \Omega$** (gives 1042 Hz/V, a 4% over-range, acceptable).

**Step 4:** Compute monostable pulse width:

$$t_{OS} = 1.1 \times 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 74.8 \, \mu s$$

**Mark allocation:** Equation setup (2 marks), solving for $R_S$ (2 marks), $R_{OS}$ choice and $t_{OS}$ calculation (2 marks), final numerical answers (1 mark).

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, B.Tech Biomedical Engineering — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **Frequency-to-Voltage (F/V) converter** using LM331. Derive the expression for output voltage in terms of input frequency and the external components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomedical pressure sensor produces a frequency output in the range **200 Hz to 2 kHz** corresponding to pressures of **0 to 250 mmHg**. Design a F/V converter using LM331 to obtain an output of **0 to 5 V** for this range. Assume $R_{OS} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_{OS} = 0.001 \, \mu F$. Calculate the required values of $R_S$ and $R_L$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The F/V converter uses the LM331's current-pump output filtered by a low-pass RC network. Each input pulse triggers a monostable, which activates the current pump for a fixed duration $t_{OS}$. The pump delivers charge packets of size $I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS}$ at rate $f_{in}$. A low-pass filter (resistor $R_L$ in series, capacitor $C_F$ in parallel) averages these packets to a DC voltage.

**Derivation:**

Average pump current: $I_{avg} = f_{in} \cdot I_{pump} \cdot t_{OS}$

Output voltage: $V_{out} = I_{avg} \cdot R_L = f_{in} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2.09 R_S} \cdot 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS} \cdot R_L$

Consolidating constants:

$$V_{out} = 2.09 \cdot f_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_L R_{OS} C_{OS}}{R_S}$$

**Mark allocation:** Block diagram (2 marks), operation (2 marks), derivation (3 marks).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute the F/V gain required.

At full scale: $f_{in,max} = 2000$ Hz must produce $V_{out} = 5$ V.

$$K_{fv} = \frac{5}{2000} = 2.5 \text{ mV/Hz} = 0.0025 \text{ V/Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the F/V transfer gain formula:

$$K_{fv} = 2.09 \cdot \frac{R_L \cdot R_{OS} \cdot C_{OS}}{R_S}$$

Substitute $R_{OS} = 10^4 \Omega$, $C_{OS} = 10^{-9}$ F, so $R_{OS} C_{OS} = 10^{-5}$ s:

$$0.0025 = 2.09 \cdot \frac{R_L \times 10^{-5}}{R_S}$$

$$\frac{R_L}{R_S} = \frac{0.0025}{2.09 \times 10^{-5}} = 119.6$$

**Step 3:** Choose a standard $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12). Then $R_L = 119.6 \text{ k}\Omega$ ≈ **$120 \text{ k}\Omega$** (E12 standard, gives 2.51 mV/Hz, 0.4% error).

**Step 4:** Verify zero-scale. At $f_{in} = 200$ Hz:

$$V_{out} = 0.00251 \times 200 = 0.502 \text{ V}$$

This non-zero offset is typical; in practice, a **DC offset nulling circuit** at the op-amp output subtracts this value to obtain true 0 V at 200 Hz.

**Mark allocation:** Gain calculation (2 marks), formula application (2 marks), component selection (2 marks), offset verification (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in V/F and F/V Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2.09 constant.** KTU examiners specifically test whether students remember the LM331 internal scaling. Losing this gives answers off by a factor of 2.09 (≈50% error).
> 2. **Confusing V/F and F/V formulas.** The numerator and denominator of $R_S$ and $R_L$ are SWAPPED between the two. A frequent error is writing $R_L/R_S$ in the V/F formula (should be $R_S/R_L$).
> 3. **Ignoring the 1.1 factor in $t_{OS}$.** The monostable pulse width is $1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS}$, **not** $R_{OS} C_{OS}$. This 10% error propagates into frequency calculations.
> 4. **Unit errors on $C_{OS}$.** Capacitor values are often in pF, nF, or µF. Always convert to FARADS before substituting into formulas.
> 5. **Not mentioning galvanic isolation.** For biomedical questions, examiners award 1–2 bonus marks for explicitly stating that V/F → optocoupler → F/V chain provides **patient isolation** — a *clinical safety* concept expected of biomedical engineers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **V/F Conversion** = Voltage → Frequency. **F/V Conversion** = Frequency → Voltage. They are **reciprocal** operations.
- **Canonical IC:** LM331 by Texas Instruments. Industry standard in PCBRL407 lab.
- **Master Formula (V/F):** $f_{out} = (V_{in}/2.09) \cdot (R_S/R_L) \cdot (1/R_{OS} C_{OS})$
- **Master Formula (F/V):** $V_{out} = 2.09 \cdot f_{in} \cdot (R_L R_{OS} C_{OS} / R_S)$
- **Monostable Pulse Width:** $t_{OS} = 1.1 R_{OS} C_{OS}$ — same formula in both directions.
- **Magic Constant:** **2.09 V** — internal scaling of LM331 bandgap reference. Do not omit.
- **Biomedical Use-Cases:** ECG/EEG/EMG patient isolation, biotelemetry, precision ADC, motor speed sensing, long-cable signal transmission.
- **Advantage over ADC:** Noise immunity, galvanic isolation, single-wire transmission, arbitrary resolution by gate-time selection.
- **Charge-Balance Principle:** Net charge on timing capacitor must be zero over a full period — this is the equilibrium condition that gives linearity.
- **Linearity Specification:** LM331 achieves 0.01% linearity (full-scale) at 10 kHz — exceptional for monolithic IC.
- **Safety Note:** In patient-connected equipment, V/F → F/V with optocoupler breaks the **galvanic path**, preventing microshock (<10 µA) that can cause ventricular fibrillation.
- **Lab Procedure Tip:** When verifying linearity in the KTU lab exam, plot $f_{out}$ vs $V_{in}$ for at least **5 input levels** (e.g., 1V, 2V, 5V, 8V, 10V) and compute the **correlation coefficient $R^2$**; it should exceed 0.999.
- **Sourcing Components:** $R_S$ sets full-scale frequency, $R_L$ sets input current range, $R_{OS}$ sets pulse width, $C_{OS}$ sets pulse width — all four are **independent** design knobs.
- **Frequency Counter Connection:** To measure $f_{out}$, connect the LM331 pulse output (pin 3) directly to a digital frequency counter or to the **INT0** pin of an 8051/Arduino for embedded counting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLL using 4046
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** PLL using 4046

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative feedback control system that synchronizes the phase and frequency of a locally generated oscillator signal (VCO output) with an incoming reference input signal. The synchronization state is called the **"Locked"** state, and the system remains locked over a finite band of frequencies known as the **Lock Range** (also called the Tracking Range or Hold-in Range).

The **CD4046** is a monolithic, low-power CMOS Micropower Phase-Locked Loop IC manufactured using RCA's CMOS technology. It contains two independent Phase Comparators (PC-I — XOR-based and PC-II — Edge-triggered JK flip-flop based), a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO), a source follower, and a Zener diode for supply regulation. It operates over a supply range of **3 V to 18 V**, making it ideal for biomedical instrumentation, signal conditioning, and frequency synthesis applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBRL407):** Students are expected to *design, build, and test* a PLL using the CD4046 IC. This includes plotting the Lock Range and Capture Range characteristics, verifying the VCO transfer curve, and observing the locking phenomenon on a CRO.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing at a playground. Your friend's swing is moving back and forth at a certain rhythm. To make your swing match your friend's swing, you must:
1. **Observe (Detect)** the timing of your friend's swing reaching the highest point.
2. **Compare** it with the timing of your own swing.
3. **Adjust** your push timing (earlier push = push harder to speed up, later push = slow down) so that the two swings now move in perfect rhythm.

This is exactly what a PLL does with *electronic signals*:
- The **friend's swing** = Reference Input Signal ($f_{in}$).
- **Your swing** = Voltage Controlled Oscillator output ($f_{out}$).
- **Observing the timing** = **Phase Comparator (PC)**.
- **Adjusting the push** = **VCO** (Voltage Controlled Oscillator).
- **Your brain's understanding** = **Low Pass Filter (LPF)**, which removes jitter and provides a smooth control voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Locking" Concept:** When the two swings (signals) are perfectly synchronized in frequency AND phase, the system is "Phase Locked." Any tiny drift in the input frequency produces a small DC correction voltage that nudges the VCO back into lock. This is why PLLs are incredibly robust at extracting clean signals from noise — a property heavily exploited in **FM demodulators, FSK modems, and biomedical telemetry receivers**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage (CD4046) | $V_{DD}$ | **3 V – 18 V (typical 9 V or 12 V)** |
| Maximum Operating Frequency | $f_{max}$ | **~1.4 MHz @ V_DD = 10 V, ~ 0.6 MHz @ 5 V** |
| VCO Linearity Error | $\varepsilon_{VCO}$ | **< 1% over full V_DD range** |
| Phase Comparator I Output Swing | $V_{PC-I}$ | **0 V to V_DD (rail-to-rail)** |
| Zener Diode Reference Voltage | $V_{Z}$ | **~5.2 V (Pin 15) when V_DD > V_Z** |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram and Operational Theory

The CD4046 PLL consists of three essential building blocks plus auxiliary components:

1. **Phase Comparator (PC):** Compares the reference input $f_{in}$ (Pin 14) with the VCO output $f_{VCO}$ (Pin 3) and produces an error voltage proportional to the phase difference $\Delta\phi$.
2. **Low Pass Filter (LPF):** A passive RC filter (external) at Pin 12 (PC-I) or Pin 13 (PC-II) removes high-frequency components, leaving a smooth DC control voltage $V_c$ (sometimes called $V_{ctrl}$).
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates a frequency $f_{VCO}$ that is a linear function of $V_c$. The VCO output is fed back to the Phase Comparator.
4. **Source Follower (Buffer):** Provides a low-impedance buffered version of $V_c$ at Pin 10 (useful for instrumentation).
5. **Zener Diode:** Internally regulated ~5.2 V reference available at Pin 15, allowing one supply to power both analog and digital 5 V logic.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Locking Mechanism

- **Step 1 — Free Running:** With $V_c = 0$ V (or at the offset level), the VCO oscillates at its **center frequency** $f_0$ (also called the free-running frequency). This is set by the external timing components $R_1$, $R_2$, and $C_1$ connected to Pins 11, 12, and 6/7.
- **Step 2 — Phase Error Generation:** When $f_{in}$ differs from $f_{VCO}$, the phase comparator generates a non-zero average output.
- **Step 3 — Filtering:** The LPF smooths this into a DC voltage $V_c$.
- **Step 4 — Frequency Correction:** $V_c$ is fed to the VCO input (Pin 9), which pushes $f_{VCO}$ toward $f_{in}$.
- **Step 5 — Lock Acquired:** When $f_{VCO} = f_{in}$ and $\Delta\phi$ is constant, the system is **Locked**. The PLL now tracks any slow drift in $f_{in}$ within the **Lock Range**.
- **Step 6 — Lock Lost:** If $f_{in}$ changes too rapidly or too far, the PLL "slips cycles" and unlocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lock Range vs. Capture Range — THE most frequently asked concept in KTU exams:**
> - **Lock Range ($2f_L$):** The range of frequencies over which the PLL *remains* locked once acquired. Determined solely by the **maximum VCO frequency** and **minimum VCO frequency** achievable — i.e., it is set by the **VCO design** ($R_1, R_2, C_1$).
> - **Capture Range ($2f_C$):** The range of frequencies over which the PLL can *acquire* lock starting from an unlocked state. Determined by the **LPF bandwidth** — narrower LPF → smaller capture range. Always **$2f_C \leq 2f_L$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{VCO} = f_{min} + K_{VCO} \cdot V_c$ | VCO Transfer Equation (linear) |
| 2 | $f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{R_2 (C_1 + 32 \text{ pF})}$ | Minimum VCO frequency (when $R_2 \neq \infty$) |
| 3 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 (C_1 + 32 \text{ pF})} + f_{min}$ | Maximum VCO frequency |
| 4 | $f_0 \text{ (center)} = \dfrac{f_{min} + f_{max}}{2}$ | Free-running / Center frequency |
| 5 | $f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | **Half Lock Range** (one-sided) |
| 6 | $2f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | **Full Lock Range** |
| 7 | $f_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi f_L}{\tau}}$ | **Capture Range** (approximate for first-order LPF) |
| 8 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | LPF Time Constant |
| 9 | $K_{VCO} = \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD} - V_{SS}}$ | VCO Gain constant (Hz/V) |
| 10 | $V_{lock} = V_c \text{ at lock}$ | Steady-state control voltage (used in FM demod.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PLLs are ubiquitous in **biomedical instrumentation**. They are used in **FM telemetry transmitters** (to stabilize carrier frequency), **ECG/EEG pacemaker clock recovery circuits**, **heart rate monitors** (to extract a clean QRS-triggered clock from a noisy bio-signal), and **motor speed control** in infusion pumps and ventilators.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the VCO Transfer Equation

The VCO in the CD4046 is a current-controlled multivibrator. The charging current $I$ through the external timing capacitor $C_1$ is set by the external resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ and the control voltage $V_c$ (Pin 9).

**Step 1:** The charging current of the timing capacitor is:
$$
I_{charge} \propto \frac{V_c}{R_1} + \frac{V_{DD}}{R_2}
$$

**Step 2:** The time period $T$ of the VCO output is twice the time required for $C_1$ to charge from $V_{SS}$ to $V_{DD}$ (since the oscillator is a relaxation-type astable):
$$
T = 2 \cdot \frac{C_1 \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{charge}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the current expression:
$$
T = 2 \cdot C_1 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{V_c \cdot R_2 + V_{DD} \cdot R_1} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** The VCO frequency is the reciprocal of the period:
$$
f_{VCO} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V_c \cdot R_2 + V_{DD} \cdot R_1}{2 \cdot C_1 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot R_1 \cdot R_2}
$$

**Step 5:** Separating into constant and variable terms:
$$
f_{VCO} = \frac{1}{2 C_1 R_1} + \frac{V_c}{2 C_1 R_2 V_{DD}}
$$

**Step 6:** Identifying the minimum frequency (when $V_c = 0$, with $R_2$ finite):
$$
f_{min} = \frac{1}{2 C_1 R_2}
$$

**Step 7:** Identifying the maximum frequency (when $V_c = V_{DD}$):
$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 C_1 R_1} + f_{min}
$$

**Step 8:** Substituting back to get the canonical linear VCO equation:
$$
\boxed{\,f_{VCO} = f_{min} + \left( \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} \right) \cdot V_c\,}
$$

> This is the **VCO Transfer Curve** — students in the KTU lab must plot this experimentally by sweeping $V_c$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ in steps and measuring $f_{VCO}$ on a frequency counter.

## 3.2 Derivation of Capture Range (First-Order Approximation)

The capture range $2 f_C$ for a first-order RC low-pass filter is derived by considering the loop's ability to "pull in" an unlocked VCO frequency to match a slowly varying input.

**Step 1:** The phase detector (PC-II) produces a beat frequency equal to the difference $\Delta f = \vert f_{in} - f_{VCO} \vert$ when unlocked.

**Step 2:** For the LPF to pass this beat frequency (so the VCO can correct it), the beat frequency must lie within the filter's bandwidth:
$$
\Delta f \leq f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

**Step 3:** The maximum initial $\Delta f$ the loop can correct is the half-capture range $f_C$. Equating:
$$
2\pi f_C \cdot R C = \text{Loop factor} \approx \sqrt{2\pi f_L \cdot R C}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the capture range:
$$
\boxed{\,f_C = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2\pi f_L}{R C}} = \sqrt{\frac{f_L}{2\pi R C}}\,}
$$

**Analytical Matrix — Phase Comparator I vs. II:**

| Feature | PC-I (XOR Gate) | PC-II (Edge-Triggered JK-FF) |
|---|---|---|
| Lock condition | $f_{in} = f_{VCO}$, 90° phase difference | $f_{in} = f_{VCO}$, 0° phase difference |
| Lock range | $2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | $2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$ |
| Capture range | Depends on LPF; **smaller than PC-II** | **Larger** capture range; depends on LPF |
| Sensitivity to harmonics | **Locks on harmonics** (unwanted) | **No harmonic lock** (preferred) |
| Output at lock | $V_c = V_{DD}/2$ (50% duty cycle XOR) | $V_c \approx 0$ V (tri-state) |
| Use case | FM demodulation | Frequency synthesis, FSK demod |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CD4046 Pin Architecture

The CD4046 is housed in a 16-pin DIP package. Below is the pin-out and functional mapping:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pVDD["Pin 16 VDD Positive Supply 3V to 18V"]
    pVSS["Pin 8 VSS Ground 0V Reference"]
    pREF["Pin 14 Signal Input Fin Reference frequency in"]
    pCOMP1["Pin 13 PC-II Output Phase Comparator II output"]
    pCOMP2["Pin 2 PC-I Output XOR phase comparator output"]
    pC1A["Pin 6 C1A Timing capacitor terminal A"]
    pC1B["Pin 7 C1B Timing capacitor terminal B"]
    pR1["Pin 11 R1 Resistor to VDD sets fmax"]
    pR2["Pin 12 R2 Resistor to VSS sets fmin"]
    pVCOIN["Pin 9 VCO Input Control voltage Vc from LPF"]
    pVCOOUT["Pin 4 VCO Output fVCO to feedback"]
    pDEMOD["Pin 10 Source Follower Buffered Vc for FM demod"]
    pZENER["Pin 15 Zener Diode 5.2V regulated output"]
    pINH["Pin 5 Inhibit High disables VCO"]
    pPC1IN["Pin 3 Comparator I Input receives fVCO"]
    pPC2IN["Pin 1 Phase Comparator II Input receives fVCO"]
    pSIGNAL["Pin 14 Signal Input Fin"]

    pVDD --> pR1
    pR1 --> pC1A
    pC1A --> pC1B
    pC1B --> pR2
    pR2 --> pVSS
    pR1 -.->|"sets Imax"| pVCOIN
    pVCOIN --> pVCOOUT
    pVCOOUT --> pPC1IN
    pVCOOUT --> pPC2IN
    pSIGNAL --> pCOMP1
    pSIGNAL --> pCOMP2
    pCOMP1 --> lpf["External LPF at Pin 13"]
    pCOMP2 --> lpf2["External LPF at Pin 2"]
    lpf --> pVCOIN
    lpf2 --> pVCOIN
```

## 4.2 Functional Block-Level PLL Architecture

The complete signal flow inside the CD4046 and its external connections:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference Input Fin\nFunction Generator"] --> B["Pin 14 Signal In"]
    B --> C{"Phase Detector\nSelection via MUX"}
    C -->|PC-I XOR| D1["Pin 2 PC-I Output"]
    C -->|PC-II JK-FF| D2["Pin 13 PC-II Output"]
    D1 --> E1["External LPF R3 C2\nSmooth to DC Vc"]
    D2 --> E2["External LPF R C\nSmooth to DC Vc"]
    E1 --> F["Pin 9 VCO Control Voltage Vc"]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G["VCO Core\nfVCO depends on R1 R2 C1"]
    G --> H["Pin 4 VCO Output fVCO"]
    H --> I["CRO Display\nor Frequency Counter"]
    H -->|Feedback Path| C
    F -.->|"Buffered"| J["Pin 10 Source Follower Output\nfor measurement"]
    K["Pin 5 Inhibit"] -.->|High disables| G
    L["Pin 15 Zener 5.2V"] -.->|Optional ref| M["External 5V Logic"]
```

## 4.3 Experimental Test Setup (Lab Wiring)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FG[Function Generator]
        FIN["f_in 1 kHz Square Wave"]
    end
    subgraph CD[CD4046 IC]
        P14["Pin 14"]
        P9["Pin 9 Vc"]
        P11["Pin 11 R1"]
        P12["Pin 12 R2"]
        P6P7["Pins 6 and 7 C1"]
        P3["Pin 3 Feedback"]
        P4["Pin 4 fVCO"]
    end
    subgraph EXT[External Components]
        R1["R1 10k ohm to VDD"]
        R2["R2 10k ohm to VSS"]
        C1["C1 1 nF Timing Cap"]
        LPF["LPF R3 100k C2 1uF"]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Outputs]
        CRO["Dual Trace CRO\nCh1 fin Ch2 fVCO"]
        FC["Frequency Counter"]
        VM["DC Voltmeter at Pin 9 Vc"]
    end

    FIN --> P14
    R1 --- P11
    R2 --- P12
    C1 --- P6P7
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> LPF
    LPF --> P9
    P4 --> CRO
    P14 --> CRO
    P4 --> FC
    P9 --> VM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip:** In the KTU lab, students typically use **Phase Comparator II (PC-II)** because it locks only on the fundamental frequency and does not produce false locks on harmonics — a critical property when the input is a noisy biomedical signal. Connect PC-II output (Pin 13) to the LPF, not Pin 2.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Define a Phase Locked Loop (PLL). List its three main building blocks and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Phase Locked Loop (PLL) is a negative feedback control system that synchronizes the output frequency of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency of an applied reference input signal, maintaining a fixed phase relationship between them.

The three main building blocks are:
1. **Phase Comparator (PC):** Compares the reference input signal with the VCO output and generates an error voltage proportional to the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ between them.
2. **Low Pass Filter (LPF):** Filters out the high-frequency AC components from the phase comparator output to produce a smooth DC control voltage $V_c$.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output frequency $f_{VCO}$ whose value is a linear function of the applied control voltage $V_c$. The VCO output is fed back to the phase comparator to close the loop.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing blocks, 1 mark for functions)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Distinguish between Lock Range and Capture Range of a PLL. Which one is always greater and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lock Range ($2f_L$):** It is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL *remains locked* once it has acquired lock. It is determined solely by the VCO design parameters $R_1, R_2, C_1$ and is equal to $f_{max} - f_{min}$.
- **Capture Range ($2f_C$):** It is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *acquire lock* starting from an unlocked (free-running) state. It depends on the LPF bandwidth — a narrower filter reduces the capture range.
- **Lock Range is always greater than or equal to Capture Range ($2f_L \geq 2f_C$):** Because once locked, the loop has a much stronger pull on the VCO (steady-state DC correction), while acquisition requires the LPF to pass the beat frequency, which limits how far off-frequency the input can be initially.

*(1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the inequality and reason)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of the CD4046 PLL IC and explain the function of each block. State any two applications of PLL in biomedical instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a PLL using CD4046 to operate with a center frequency $f_0 = 10$ kHz and lock range $2f_L = 8$ kHz. Assume $V_{DD} = 10$ V, $C_1 = 1$ nF, and $R_1 = R_2$. Calculate the required values of $R_1$, $R_2$, $C_1$, and choose a suitable LPF for a capture range of approximately 2 kHz. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
The CD4046 block diagram is shown in SECTION 4.2 above (drawn by the student). The functions are:
- **Phase Comparator I (XOR gate):** Produces an output whose average DC value is proportional to the phase difference between the two inputs. The XOR output is a square wave whose duty cycle varies from 0% to 100% as the phase error varies from 0° to 180°.
- **Phase Comparator II (Edge-triggered JK flip-flop):** Produces a tri-state output (high, low, or high-impedance) that is proportional to the phase and frequency error. It locks at 0° phase difference and does not lock on harmonics.
- **VCO:** Generates a frequency that is linearly proportional to the control voltage $V_c$ at Pin 9. The frequency range is set by $R_1, R_2, C_1$.
- **Source Follower:** Provides a buffered, low-impedance output of $V_c$ at Pin 10 for measurement or demodulation.
- **Zener Diode:** Provides a regulated 5.2 V reference at Pin 15, useful for powering external 5 V logic from the same supply.

**Two applications in biomedical instrumentation:**
1. **FM demodulator in cardiac telemetry:** A PLL is used to recover the original ECG waveform from an FM-modulated RF carrier transmitted wirelessly from a patient's body.
2. **Clock recovery in pacemaker synchronization:** A PLL extracts a clean timing clock from a noisy incoming bio-signal to synchronize pacemaker stimulation pulses with natural cardiac activity.

*(Block diagram 2 marks, explanation 3 marks, applications 2 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the specifications:**
$$
f_0 = 10 \text{ kHz}, \quad 2f_L = 8 \text{ kHz}, \quad V_{DD} = 10 \text{ V}, \quad C_1 = 1 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the VCO frequency limits:**
$$
f_{max} = f_0 + f_L = 10 + 4 = 14 \text{ kHz}
$$
$$
f_{min} = f_0 - f_L = 10 - 4 = 6 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_1$ (using the $f_{max}$ formula):**
$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1} + f_{min} \quad (\text{approximation for } R_1 \ll R_2)
$$
$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{C_1 (f_{max} - f_{min})} = \frac{1}{10^{-9} \times 8 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-6}}
$$
$$
R_1 = 125 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_2$ (using the $f_{min}$ formula):**
$$
f_{min} = \frac{1}{R_2 C_1} \implies R_2 = \frac{1}{C_1 \cdot f_{min}} = \frac{1}{10^{-9} \times 6 \times 10^3}
$$
$$
R_2 = 166.67 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 167 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Choose LPF for $2f_C = 2$ kHz (so $f_C = 1$ kHz):**
Using the capture range formula:
$$
f_C = \sqrt{\frac{f_L}{2\pi R C}}
$$
$$
R C = \frac{f_L}{2\pi f_C^2} = \frac{4000}{2\pi \times (1000)^2} = 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$
Choose $C_2 = 1 \mu F$, then $R_3 = 636.6 \ \Omega \approx 680 \ \Omega$ (standard value).

**Final Design Summary:**
- $R_1 = 125$ k$\Omega$ (use 120 k$\Omega$ standard)
- $R_2 = 167$ k$\Omega$ (use 150 k$\Omega$ standard + 17 k$\Omega$ series)
- $C_1 = 1$ nF
- LPF: $R_3 = 680 \ \Omega$, $C_2 = 1 \ \mu F$

*(Calculations 5 marks, LPF design 1 mark, final summary 1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PLL. Derive the expression for the VCO transfer function $f_{VCO}$ in terms of the control voltage $V_c$, resistors $R_1, R_2$, and capacitor $C_1$ for the CD4046 IC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An experiment is conducted with a CD4046 PLL where $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 50$ k$\Omega$, and $C_1 = 1$ nF at $V_{DD} = 10$ V. The control voltage is measured as $V_c = 6$ V at lock. Calculate: (i) $f_{min}$, (ii) $f_{max}$, (iii) $f_{VCO}$ at lock, and (iv) the VCO gain constant $K_{VCO}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
*(Block diagram from Section 4.2 to be drawn by the student — 2 marks)*

**Operation explanation (3 marks):** The reference signal $f_{in}$ and VCO output $f_{VCO}$ are fed to the phase comparator. The PC generates an error voltage $V_e$ proportional to the phase difference $\Delta\phi = \phi_{in} - \phi_{VCO}$. The LPF removes the high-frequency AC ripple and produces a clean DC control voltage $V_c$. This $V_c$ adjusts the VCO frequency to minimize the phase error. When the loop is locked, $f_{VCO} = f_{in}$ and $\Delta\phi$ is constant. Any drift in $f_{in}$ is corrected by the loop, providing frequency tracking.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1 above:
$$
\boxed{\,f_{VCO} = \frac{1}{2 C_1 R_1} + \frac{V_c}{2 C_1 R_2 V_{DD}} = f_{min} + \frac{(f_{max} - f_{min})}{V_{DD}} \cdot V_c\,}
$$

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 50$ k$\Omega$, $C_1 = 1$ nF, $V_{DD} = 10$ V, $V_c = 6$ V.

**(i) Calculate $f_{min}$ (when $V_c = 0$ V):**
$$
f_{min} = \frac{1}{2 R_2 C_1} = \frac{1}{2 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{10^{-4}}
$$
$$
\boxed{f_{min} = 10 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(ii) Calculate $f_{max}$ (when $V_c = V_{DD} = 10$ V):**
$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 R_1 C_1} + f_{min} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-9}} + 10^4
$$
$$
f_{max} = 50 \text{ kHz} + 10 \text{ kHz}
$$
$$
\boxed{f_{max} = 60 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(iii) Calculate $f_{VCO}$ at lock with $V_c = 6$ V:**
$$
f_{VCO} = f_{min} + \frac{(f_{max} - f_{min})}{V_{DD}} \cdot V_c = 10^4 + \frac{(60 \times 10^3 - 10^4)}{10} \times 6
$$
$$
f_{VCO} = 10^4 + \frac{50 \times 10^3}{10} \times 6 = 10^4 + 5 \times 10^3 \times 6
$$
$$
f_{VCO} = 10^4 + 30 \times 10^3
$$
$$
\boxed{f_{VCO} = 40 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(iv) Calculate the VCO gain constant $K_{VCO}$:**
$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} = \frac{(60 - 10) \times 10^3}{10}
$$
$$
\boxed{K_{VCO} = 5000 \text{ Hz/V} = 5 \text{ kHz/V}}
$$

*(Each sub-part: ~1.75 marks; showing substitution = 0.5 mark, final numerical answer = 0.5 mark, units = 0.5 mark, accuracy = 0.25 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_{max}$ formula:** Many students incorrectly write $f_{max} = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1}$ forgetting the $+f_{min}$ term. The correct formula is $f_{max} = f_{min} + \frac{1}{R_1 C_1}$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up Lock Range and Capture Range definitions:** Lock range is set by the VCO (independent of LPF); Capture range is set by the LPF. Writing "Lock range depends on R and C of LPF" will be marked wrong. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the 32 pF parasitic capacitance** in the VCO timing formula (the CD4046 datasheet includes it). For lab-scale values of $C_1 \geq 1$ nF, the 32 pF is negligible, but mentioning it shows depth. **Loss: 0–1 mark.**
> 4. **Not drawing Pin numbers on the block diagram:** Always label Pins 14, 4, 9, 2/13, 11, 12, 6/7 on your diagram — KTU evaluators specifically check this. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Unit errors in numerical answers:** $K_{VCO}$ must be expressed in Hz/V, not in kHz/V (or convert consistently). Mismatch in units is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ PLL = **Phase Comparator + Low Pass Filter + VCO** in a negative-feedback loop.
- ✅ **CD4046** is a CMOS PLL IC with two phase comparators, a VCO, a source follower, and a 5.2 V Zener reference.
- ✅ **Phase Comparator I (XOR)** locks at 90° phase shift; **Phase Comparator II (JK-FF)** locks at 0° phase shift and does **not** lock on harmonics.
- ✅ **Lock Range** is set by the VCO: $2 f_L = f_{max} - f_{min}$.
- ✅ **Capture Range** is set by the LPF bandwidth: narrower LPF → smaller capture range.
- ✅ **Always:** $2 f_L \geq 2 f_C$ (Lock Range $\geq$ Capture Range).
- ✅ VCO transfer equation: $f_{VCO} = f_{min} + \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}} \cdot V_c$.
- ✅ $f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2 R_2 C_1}$ ; $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 R_1 C_1} + f_{min}$.
- ✅ VCO gain $K_{VCO} = \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}}$ (units: Hz/V).
- ✅ **Pin 14** = Reference Input, **Pin 4** = VCO Output, **Pin 9** = VCO Control Input, **Pins 6 & 7** = $C_1$, **Pins 11 & 12** = $R_1$ and $R_2$.
- ✅ **Biomedical Applications:** FM telemetry demodulation, pacemaker clock recovery, heart-rate extraction, infusion pump motor speed control, EEG signal synchronization.
- ✅ In the KTU lab experiment, **always use PC-II** (Pin 13 output → LPF → Pin 9) for robust locking on fundamental frequency, especially with noisy bio-signals.
- ✅ **Lab Measurement Procedure:** Sweep $V_c$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ in steps of 1 V, measure $f_{VCO}$ at each step → plot VCO transfer curve → verify linearity.
- ✅ **Lock Range Measurement:** Increase $f_{in}$ slowly from $f_0$ until lock is lost (this is $f_{max\,lock}$); then decrease $f_{in}$ until lock is lost (this is $f_{min\,lock}$). Then $2 f_L = f_{max\,lock} - f_{min\,lock}$.
- ✅ **Capture Range Measurement:** Start unlocked; slowly vary $f_{in}$ from $f_0$ until PLL suddenly locks; record the value. This is the capture limit.
- ✅ **Safety:** Never exceed $V_{DD} = 18$ V on the CD4046; use the Zener at Pin 15 to derive 5 V for any external TTL logic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash ADC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL407 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flash ADC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flash Analog-to-Digital Converter (Flash ADC)**, also known as a **Parallel ADC**, is the fastest topology of analog-to-digital conversion in which the entire conversion process is completed in a **single clock cycle** (one comparator decision time). It achieves this by employing a parallel bank of $2^N - 1$ comparators that simultaneously compare the input analog voltage against $2^N - 1$ equally spaced reference voltages generated by a precision resistor ladder network. The thermometer code output from the comparators is then passed through a priority encoder to produce the final N-bit digital output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In biomedical electronics, the Flash ADC is studied as a critical interface component for digitizing high-bandwidth bioelectric signals (ECG, EMG, EEG, neural spikes) where conversion latency must be on the order of nanoseconds.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Audience Polling" Intuition

Imagine a stadium of $2^N - 1$ judges sitting in a tiered auditorium. Each judge is assigned a fixed height threshold. When a contestant walks on stage, **every single judge simultaneously looks at the contestant and decides independently**: "Is the contestant taller than my assigned height line?" Judges below the contestant's height say YES, judges above say NO. After a single glance (one parallel decision), the **highest YES judge** instantly identifies the contestant's height bracket.

- **Judges** $\rightarrow$ Comparators
- **Height thresholds on each tier** $\rightarrow$ Reference voltages from the resistor ladder
- **YES/NO response pattern** $\rightarrow$ Thermometer code (consecutive 1s followed by consecutive 0s)
- **Highest YES judge** $\rightarrow$ Priority encoder logic
- **Time for the audience to look** $\rightarrow$ One comparator propagation delay (single clock cycle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Flash"?** The conversion is so fast it appears to happen instantaneously — like a camera flash capturing the analog voltage in a single instant.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are mandatory for any KTU numerical problem on Flash ADCs:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of output bits | $N$ | dimensionless |
| Number of comparators required | $N_c$ | $2^N - 1$ |
| Full-scale input range | $V_{FS}$ | **Volts (V)** |
| Least Significant Bit (Resolution) | $\Delta V$ or **LSB** | **Volts (V)** |
| Reference voltage (typically) | $V_{REF}$ | **Volts (V)** |
| Clock frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| Conversion time | $T_{conv}$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Sampling rate (theoretical max) | $f_s$ | **samples/second (S/s)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Resolution (LSB):** The smallest distinguishable analog voltage step that can be resolved by the ADC, defined as $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$.
